source_label: Revision of Commonly Used Loop Knots Efficiencies
text:
# Evidence digest: controlled static tests of eight loop-knot families (2020)

## Use boundary: laboratory evidence, not a safety recipe

This is a manually curated digest of **expert-tied, deliberately dressed loop knots tested slowly to rupture on synthetic polyamide low-stretch kernmantle rope**. “Expert-tied” here describes the authors’ controlled research setting, their stated emphasis on correct tying and dressing, and their photographic documentation; the paper does not identify every tyer or quantify between-tyer variation. The evidence is static and specimen-specific. [APP-138-404: abstract; introduction; §2 Material and Methods]

The study does not validate natural-fiber rope, bondage rope, body contact, dynamic loading, cyclic loading, uplines, or human suspension. It does not supply a safe-knot rule, a load or rating recipe, retirement guidance, or permission to transplant a laboratory ranking into another rope system. The authors themselves identify low-speed static loading, low-stretch rope, and a restricted set of loop knots as boundaries requiring further research. [APP-138-404: §3.4 Future Research]

Numerical knot-efficiency values and rankings are intentionally absent. They would look portable even though the paper’s central statistical finding is that efficiency changes with the rope population being tested. Source figures, source tables, tying diagrams, standards text, trademarks, vendor claims, equipment brands, and operational recommendations are also excluded. [APP-138-404: introduction; §3.3.1; §3.3.4; §3.3.9]

## Article identity

The source is J. Šimon, V. Dekýš, and P. Palček, “Revision of Commonly Used Loop Knots Efficiencies,” *Acta Physica Polonica A*, volume 138, number 3, 2020, printed pages 404–420. It records receipt on November 15, 2019 and acceptance on April 24, 2020. The DOI is `10.12693/APhysPolA.138.404`. [APP-138-404: title page]

The current publisher page states that all articles in the journal are licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International. This digest is an adaptation: it changes selection, order, wording, and emphasis; attributes the authors and source; links the DOI and license in the attribution notice; and makes no endorsement claim. [APPA-ABOUT: Open Access and Creative Commons license; CC-BY-4.0: Attribution requirements]

## The tested material, preserved without trademarks

The authors tested polyamide low-stretch kernmantle ropes that the source classified under two types of the EN 1891 standard, plus a polyamide accessory cord classified under EN 564. Naming those classifications identifies the specimens; no standards requirements or manufacturer declarations are reproduced. The article reports a mix of new and previously used ropes made by similar technology. [APP-138-404: §2.1 Ropes; Tables I–II]

The exact neutral specimen inventory is:

source_label: Revision of Commonly Used Loop Knots Efficiencies
text:
- **G:** type A low-stretch kernmantle rope, 10.5 mm, manufactured in 2015, new and unused. This was the common reference rope on which all eight knot families were tested. [APP-138-404: Table I; §3.3.9]
- **B:** type A low-stretch kernmantle rope, 10.5 mm, manufactured in 2006. The table supplies no mission or wear-stage entry for this specimen. [APP-138-404: Table I]
- **EW:** type A low-stretch kernmantle rope, 10.5 mm, manufactured in 2014, previously used for work at height while painting vertical structures; the source records color affecting both sheath and core. [APP-138-404: Table I]
- **L1:** type A low-stretch kernmantle rope, 11.0 mm, manufactured in 2006, previously used for speleological rescue; the table supplies no separate wear-stage entry. [APP-138-404: Table I]
- **ER:** type A low-stretch kernmantle rope, 10.5 mm, manufactured in 2008, previously used for speleological rescue; its sheath was worn but the source reported no defect penetrating to the core. [APP-138-404: Table I]
- **L2:** type B low-stretch kernmantle rope, 9.1 mm, manufactured in 2004, previously used for mountain rescue; the table supplies no separate wear-stage entry. [APP-138-404: Table I]
- **G8:** polyamide accessory cord, 8 mm, manufactured during 2015–2018, new and unused. It was used only in a restricted cross-load comparison alongside one rope. [APP-138-404: Table II; §3.3.8]

The codes above are the article’s abbreviations, not product recommendations. Manufacturer names, product trademarks, declared breaking strengths, impact-force values, fall counts, mass, elongation, shrinkage, sheath-slip values, and construction recipes are omitted. A manufacturing year or prior mission is a specimen-history field, not evidence for a storage life, retirement interval, or present safety condition. [APP-138-404: Tables I–II; §2.1 Ropes]

The article says its ropes spanned roughly one to twelve years of age at testing and included work-at-height, speleological-rescue, and mountain-rescue histories. Those histories were not randomized treatments, and the specimens differed in diameter, construction type, wear, and manufacturer. The old-versus-new observations therefore cannot isolate calendar age from use history or other rope properties. This digest retains the inventory but excludes the paper’s storage and lifespan claims. [APP-138-404: §2.1 Ropes; §3.1 Rope Without a Knot]

## Measurement and tying controls

The tests used a horizontal, hydraulic tensile apparatus in stable laboratory conditions. Opposed cylindrical friction grips held the rope without a knot at the grip; for relevant loop tests a polished steel ring represented the attachment. The moving grip advanced at 180 mm per minute, making this a slow-static displacement test rather than an impact or fall-arrest simulation. [APP-138-404: §2.2 Tensile Tests]

Force was measured with load sensors connected to a control system. The machine was independently calibrated every two years, and the stated relative measurement error was below two tenths of one percent over the force range used. Calibration limits instrument error; it does not remove specimen variability, tying variability, fixture effects, or model error. [APP-138-404: §2.2 Tensile Tests]

The authors used one methodology across the knot families, emphasized correct tying and dressing, and retained photographic documentation. They distinguished loading direction, the I and O dressed geometries, and rupture from untying. The paper does not report a blinded tying protocol, named tyers, inter-tyer replication, a quantified dressing force, or a direct experiment on dressing quality. Its future-research section explicitly says the effect of improper dressing had not been seriously measured. [APP-138-404: introduction; terminology; §3.4 Future Research]

## Why repeated measurements and ratio statistics matter

source_label: Revision of Commonly Used Loop Knots Efficiencies
text:
Straight-rope breaking strength varied substantially between nearby pieces of nominally the same rope. The authors therefore treated both unknotted and knotted breaking strengths as distributions rather than fixed numbers. A single rupture result cannot characterize either population, and comparisons assembled from different ropes or test methods are not exchangeable. [APP-138-404: introduction; §3.1 Rope Without a Knot]

Knot efficiency is a ratio: the breaking strength of a knotted specimen divided by the breaking strength of its unknotted rope population. The two inputs are directly measured, but the efficiency distribution is derived. The authors modeled each strength population, derived the distribution of their ratio, and used a central confidence interval from that ratio distribution. Because the ratio distribution is asymmetric, the mean efficiency is not generally the ratio of the two sample means. [APP-138-404: §3.2 Statistical Properties of Knot Efficiency]

This distinction is not cosmetic. A ratio-of-means shortcut can bias the result, especially when either strength population is variable. The paper’s uncertainty intervals and replication counts must remain attached to each rope, knot, geometry, and loading comparison. Results from separate specimen populations cannot be combined merely because they share a knot name. [APP-138-404: introduction; §3.2 Statistical Properties of Knot Efficiency]

For the figure-eight and overhand families, the authors found evidence that measured efficiency decreased as the tested rope’s unknotted static breaking strength increased. They did not establish this relationship for every loop knot, bend, or hitch. The finding directly defeats the idea of one context-free efficiency number for a knot. [APP-138-404: §3.3.1 Figure Eight Loop; §3.3.4 Overhand Loop; §3.4 Future Research]

## Loading and geometry labels

In the paper’s **standard-load** arrangement, opposing forces acted through the standing part and the loop. In **cross-load**, the principal opposition was between the standing part and working end, leaving the loop comparatively lightly loaded and changing the internal stress path. These are experimental boundary conditions, not instructions for loading a knot in practice. [APP-138-404: §3.3 Loop Knots in General]

The authors also separated two dressed forms called **geometry I** and **geometry O**. The labels encode whether the standing part or working end occupies the inside position where they turn around the loop collar. They are structural labels for already dressed specimens, not step-by-step tying directions. Some knot families permit both forms; for the bowline as tested, the paper did not apply the I/O distinction. [APP-138-404: §3.3 Loop Knots in General; §3.3.5 Bowline]

The paper additionally used **ring-load** for forces applied through the loop. This condition matters because a specimen may reorganize and untie rather than rupture. Combining rupture and untying into one “strength” endpoint would compare different failure processes. [APP-138-404: introduction; §3.3 Loop Knots in General; §3.3.7 Bowline on a Bight]

## Eight loop-knot families and the bounded evidence

### 1. Figure-eight loop

The figure-eight loop was tested under standard load in I and O geometries and under cross-load. The I/O standard-load comparison used 81 measurements across five ropes and did not resolve a difference between geometries at the authors’ chosen significance level. A broader standard-load program used eleven ropes and revealed enough between-rope variation to motivate the efficiency-versus-rope-strength relationship. These results describe the tested populations, not a universal retained-strength value. [APP-138-404: §3.3.1 Figure Eight Loop]

### 2. Double figure-eight loop

The double figure-eight loop was tested under standard load in both geometries. The I/O comparison used 22 measurements on one rope and did not resolve a geometry difference. A 44-measurement comparison on that same rope did not distinguish the double-loop family from the reference figure-eight condition. One-rope equivalence at the experiment’s resolution is not proof of equivalence on other ropes or under other loading. [APP-138-404: §3.3.2 Double Figure Eight Loop]

### 3. Figure-nine loop

The figure-nine loop was tested under standard load in I and O geometries. A set of 19 measurements on one rope produced a detectable geometry difference. The authors themselves called for independent verification with multiple ropes. The source’s numerical ranking and operational recommendation are excluded because the geometry result is based on one rope population and slow-static loading. [APP-138-404: §3.3.3 Figure Nine Loop]

source_label: Revision of Commonly Used Loop Knots Efficiencies
text:
### 4. Overhand loop

The overhand loop was tested under standard load in both geometries and under cross-load. Its I/O comparison used 24 measurements on one rope and found a detectable geometry difference, again with a call for independent multi-rope verification. A wider standard-load analysis used 108 measurements across five ropes; high dispersion in used-rope results prevented some distinctions. A separate cross-load comparison with the figure-eight family used 48 measurements across three ropes and did not resolve a difference at the chosen threshold. [APP-138-404: §3.3.4 Overhand Loop]

### 5. Bowline

The bowline was tested under standard load and ring-load. In the ring-load tests it did not provide a rupture endpoint: the knot untied. Once the specimen outcome is untying, a rupture-based efficiency calculation is not the relevant statistic. The paper’s force thresholds and life-critical recommendations are excluded. [APP-138-404: §3.3.5 Bowline; §3.3.7 Bowline on a Bight]

### 6. Left-hand bowline

The left-hand bowline was likewise tested under standard load and ring-load. It also untied under ring-load rather than breaking. Its untying process differed quantitatively from the bowline’s, but those force values are not portable ratings and are omitted. The experiment supports keeping “untied” distinct from “broke”; it does not establish safe use under ring-load or cyclic conditions. [APP-138-404: §3.3.6 Left-Hand Bowline; §3.3.7 Bowline on a Bight]

### 7. Bowline on a bight

The bowline on a bight was tested under standard load in geometry I. A 51-measurement comparison did not distinguish the standard-load efficiencies of the bowline, left-hand bowline, and bowline on a bight at the selected threshold. A larger comparison against the reference family drew on 101 measurements across three ropes, but the numerical differences and rankings are excluded. The limited tested geometry and rope populations remain part of the result. [APP-138-404: §3.3.7 Bowline on a Bight]

### 8. Alpine butterfly loop

The alpine butterfly loop was tested only under cross-load. Its main comparison used 32 measurements on one rope and one accessory cord; a narrower direct comparison used 18 measurements on the same rope. Because the family lacked a standard-load program and broad multi-rope replication, neither the paper’s ranking nor its field recommendation is retained. [APP-138-404: §3.3.8 Alpine Butterfly Knot]

## Infrared and electron-microscopy observations

The authors recorded rupture with high-speed infrared imaging and separately measured the emissivity of the whole reference rope and its core so that camera signal could be interpreted as surface temperature. Before rupture, the warmest observed region of standard-loaded loops was near the entry of the standing part. At or immediately after rupture, localized surface temperatures approached the melting region of polyamide. [APP-138-404: §2.3 High-Speed Infrared Thermal Imaging; §3.3.10 Electron Microscopy and Thermal Imaging]

The temporal resolution was not sufficient to determine whether the rapid temperature rise caused rupture, rupture caused the rise, or both processes interacted. The article explicitly leaves that causal order unresolved. Therefore the thermal observation is a near-failure mechanism clue, not evidence for a usable temperature limit, inspection threshold, or body-contact prediction. [APP-138-404: §3.3.10 Electron Microscopy and Thermal Imaging]

Electron microscopy compared intact polyamide microfilaments with filaments torn in tensile tests. Intact fibers appeared cylindrical; broken ends were thickened and showed flake-like surface structure. The authors proposed that rapid heating, deformation, friction, and cooling could form those ends, but presented the explanation as a hypothesis. Product-specific microscope settings, images, and dimensional measurements are excluded. [APP-138-404: §2.4 Electron Microscopy; §3.3.10 Electron Microscopy and Thermal Imaging]

## What the experiment does not establish

The paper covers loop knots, not bends, hitches, or friction hitches. It focuses on low-stretch polyamide kernmantle ropes and one accessory cord, not dynamic climbing rope or natural fiber. Its grip moved slowly; the effect of realistic faster or impact loading remained unknown. Diameter, wetness, knotability, sheath smoothness, dressing quality, and other candidate variables were not systematically isolated. [APP-138-404: §3.4 Future Research]

source_label: Revision of Commonly Used Loop Knots Efficiencies
text:
The static-breaking-strength relationship was demonstrated only for the figure-eight and overhand families. Several geometry findings came from one rope; the alpine-butterfly program used only one rope and one cord; all-family comparisons shared a single new reference rope. Ring-load untying is a different endpoint from rupture, and comments in the article about cyclic behavior do not turn these slow-static tests into cyclic validation. [APP-138-404: §3.3.1–§3.3.9; §3.4 Future Research]

The study is valuable as a model of careful classification: identify the exact rope population and history, control dressing and loading geometry, calibrate the apparatus, replicate, propagate ratio uncertainty, and record the failure mode. It is not a lookup table for selecting a knot or assigning a retained-strength percentage. [APP-138-404: introduction; §2 Material and Methods; §3.2 Statistical Properties]

## Attribution and adaptation notice

Adapted from J. Šimon, V. Dekýš, and P. Palček, “Revision of Commonly Used Loop Knots Efficiencies,” *Acta Physica Polonica A* 138 (2020), 404–420, DOI [10.12693/APhysPolA.138.404](https://doi.org/10.12693/APhysPolA.138.404). The publisher states that the article is licensed under [CC BY 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/). This digest changes wording, order, selection, and emphasis; it excludes the source’s figures, tables, trademarks, standards text, vendor claims, formulas, numerical efficiencies, and operational recommendations. No endorsement by the authors, journal, institutions, vendors, or Creative Commons is implied.

## Citation key

- **APP-138-404** — the 2020 article identified above, official publisher PDF, manually reviewed in full.
- **APPA-ABOUT** — the journal’s current official about page, accessed July 16, 2026.
- **CC-BY-4.0** — the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International deed, accessed July 16, 2026.

No source body, figure, table, formula set, or extracted full text is stored in this corpus.

source_label: Coir ropes as a low-tech circular alternative to synthetic ropes in French Polynesia pearl farming
text:
# Evidence and limits: coir fibers, finished ropes, and French Polynesian pearl farming

## Source identity, license, and setting

Source evidence: Louis Le Gué, Peter Davies, Mael Arhant, Benoit Vincent, and Benoit Parnaudeau authored *Coir ropes as a low-tech circular alternative to synthetic ropes in French Polynesia pearl farming*. It appeared in *Cleaner and Circular Bioeconomy*, volume 9, article 100111, with DOI `10.1016/j.clcb.2024.100111`. The version of record says it became available online on 5 October 2024, and the DOI metadata places it in the December 2024 issue. [IFREMER-COIR-ROPE-2024-PDF, p. 1; DOI-CSL-COIR-ROPE-2024]

Source evidence: The article identifies copyright in the authors and is marked open access under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 license. This digest is a shortened paraphrase, not a reproduction of the paper’s figures, tables, images, experimental values, procedures, or reference list. [IFREMER-COIR-ROPE-2024-PDF, p. 1; DOI-CSL-COIR-ROPE-2024]

Source evidence: The study’s application context was local coir-rope production for pearl farming in French Polynesia, using coconut husks associated with copra cultivation. That setting bounds the evidence; the article did not study general rope use or establish rules for other environments. [IFREMER-COIR-ROPE-2024-PDF, pp. 1–2]

## Fibers, rope construction, and water state

Source evidence: The study treated individual coir fibers and finished coir ropes as distinct test objects. Its finished-rope set included machine-twisted and hand-braided constructions. Results at the fiber scale and results from an assembled rope therefore answer different questions. [IFREMER-COIR-ROPE-2024-PDF, pp. 2–3]

Source evidence: The authors compared both individual fibers and finished ropes in dry and water-saturated laboratory states. “Water-saturated” here names a study condition; it does not describe every wet, immersed, weathered, contaminated, or in-service rope. [IFREMER-COIR-ROPE-2024-PDF, pp. 3–7]

Source evidence: Within the tested individual fibers, water saturation did not produce a statistically significant change in tensile strength, although other mechanical responses changed. This is a result for the tested fibers and conditions, not a universal material guarantee. [IFREMER-COIR-ROPE-2024-PDF, pp. 4–5]

Source evidence: Finished-rope behavior did not simply reproduce the individual-fiber result. The tested machine-twisted ropes lost more rope-level performance after saturation even though the individual-fiber tensile-strength result was comparatively stable. The study therefore demonstrates a fiber-to-rope prediction limit: an isolated-fiber result cannot by itself predict an assembled rope. [IFREMER-COIR-ROPE-2024-PDF, pp. 5–7]

Source evidence: In this sample set, construction and braiding quality affected stress distribution, rupture behavior, and retention of mechanical performance after saturation. The hand-braided ropes also differed from one another, so a simple “hand-braided” or “machine-twisted” label was not by itself a performance predictor. Fiber length alone likewise did not explain the observed rope behavior. [IFREMER-COIR-ROPE-2024-PDF, pp. 5–7]

Source evidence: The authors interpreted water between fibers as potentially making inter-fiber sliding easier, which could help explain why a finished rope changed even when individual-fiber tensile strength did not. The paper presents this as a rope-structure interpretation, not as a universally established mechanism for every coir rope. [IFREMER-COIR-ROPE-2024-PDF, p. 7]

## Study-specific process result and missing evidence

Source evidence: Across the particular rope batches and retting conditions tested, the authors found no distinctive trend or statistically significant change in the measured rope mechanical properties attributable to retting. This null result is specific to the study design; it does not evaluate every retting method, material source, production route, or outcome. [IFREMER-COIR-ROPE-2024-PDF, pp. 7–8]

source_label: Coir ropes as a low-tech circular alternative to synthetic ropes in French Polynesia pearl farming
text:
Source boundary: The paper supplies no retting, soaking, extraction, drying, sorting, twisting, braiding, or testing recipe here. Its study-specific retting result cannot be converted into a process instruction or a general recommendation to perform, omit, or modify retting. [IFREMER-COIR-ROPE-2024-PDF, pp. 2–8]

Source evidence: The work did not report in-situ aging of coir ropes in pearl-farming lagoons. The authors identify service-environment stressors and long-duration behavior as missing evidence and say in-situ aging is needed before persistence over the required cultivation period can be known. Laboratory tensile observations therefore do not establish service life. [IFREMER-COIR-ROPE-2024-PDF, pp. 8–9]

Source evidence: The life-cycle assessment covered production and transport only. It excluded use and end-of-life because those data were unavailable, and the authors cautioned that generalized life-cycle data may not accurately represent a remote archipelago. The study therefore does not supply a complete cradle-to-end-of-life comparison. [IFREMER-COIR-ROPE-2024-PDF, pp. 8–9]

## Disclosures and availability

Source evidence: The PDF contains no dedicated funding section or funding-source statement, and the reviewed DOI metadata has no funder field. That absence is not evidence that the work received no funding; it records only what these two source surfaces disclose. [IFREMER-COIR-ROPE-2024-PDF, pp. 10–11; DOI-CSL-COIR-ROPE-2024]

Source evidence: The competing-interest statement says Polyacht supplied materials and that author Benoit Parnaudeau was a Polyacht employee. It also says those affiliations did not influence the study’s design, interpretation, or reporting. This is a disclosure, not independent verification of absence of influence. [IFREMER-COIR-ROPE-2024-PDF, p. 10]

Source evidence: The article’s data-availability statement says data will be made available on request. It does not identify an open repository or state that the data accompany this digest. [IFREMER-COIR-ROPE-2024-PDF, p. 10]

## Absolute non-transfer boundary

Source boundary: Nothing retained here rates, selects, certifies, approves, or recommends a rope or product. No laboratory result, construction label, wet-retention observation, retting result, or life-cycle boundary establishes current quality, safety, remaining life, strength, working load, or suitability for a different task. [IFREMER-COIR-ROPE-2024-PDF, pp. 1–10]

Source boundary: This study cannot be transferred to jute, hemp, rope hygiene, cleaning, drying, storage, care, retirement, body contact, knots, frictions, restraint, body lowering, working loads, uplines, anchors, hardpoints, bondage, or human suspension. It supplies no instruction or safety rule for any of those subjects. [IFREMER-COIR-ROPE-2024-PDF, pp. 1–11]

Source boundary: CC BY permits reuse with attribution, license notice, and an indication of changes. This digest attributes the authors, title, journal, DOI, and license; identifies itself as a narrowed paraphrase created on 16 July 2026; and does not imply endorsement by the authors, Ifremer, Elsevier, Polyacht, or Creative Commons. [IFREMER-COIR-ROPE-2024-PDF, p. 1; DOI-CSL-COIR-ROPE-2024]

source_label: How safe is BDSM? A literature review on fatal outcome in BDSM play
text:
# Reading a forensic review of reported BDSM fatalities without inventing a risk rate

Source: Anouk Schori, Christian Jackowski, and Corinna A. Schön, “How safe is BDSM? A literature review on fatal outcome in BDSM play,” *International Journal of Legal Medicine* 136, 287–295, first published 12 August 2021, DOI 10.1007/s00414-021-02674-0, PMCID PMC8813685, PMID 34383118.

License and adaptation notice: the article is licensed CC BY 4.0. This is a manually written, attributed, safety-focused adaptation. It reorganizes and critiques the evidence, corrects the source's “BDMS” typographical slips, omits case narratives and technique detail, and marks statements that the review's design cannot support.

## Scope and safety boundary

This article is a forensic literature review of published deaths reported in connection with partnered BDSM activity. It is not a cohort study of BDSM participants, a surveillance study, a population survey, a clinical guideline, or a rope-technique manual. Its 17 cases are a set of cases found in publications, not a denominator of people, scenes, rope sessions, breath-control events, or hours of exposure. Nothing in the paper establishes the probability that a particular BDSM activity will cause death. (Schori et al., 2021, Abstract; Methods; Results; Strengths and limitations)

The review's selected reports are dominated by fatal neck-compression and asphyxia events. That supports a narrow safety conclusion: external neck compression, airway or oxygen restriction, and intentional loss of consciousness can have fatal outcomes, including when participants have prior experience. It does not support the claim that ordinary rope bondage in general has the same risk profile, nor does it provide a safer way to perform breath control. The source itself says the health risks of breath-control activity cannot be eliminated. (Discussion—BDSM activities; Results—Cause of death)

No case narrative, apparatus layout, body position, sequence of failure, or reconstruction is retained here. Such detail adds little to the valid inference and could become an unsafe instructional template. The useful material is the review method, aggregate counts, category distinctions, missingness, and the limits on causal or prevalence claims.

## The question the review attempted to answer

The authors sought published reports of non-natural deaths associated with consensually described BDSM activity involving more than one person. They were interested in reported causes of death, practitioner experience and education, and community safeguards. Papers solely about autoerotic death were excluded because those events involve a different participation structure. (Methods)

That scope creates several boundaries:

- **Partnered, consensually described BDSM cases** were the intended case class.
- **Autoerotic fatalities** were excluded from case collection, although the discussion compares them with the partnered reports.
- **Natural deaths during sexual activity** were discussed as a comparison but were not part of the 17-case set.
- **Non-natural deaths associated with sexual activity** formed a broader category in one included German postmortem study.
- **Published reports** were the discovery frame; the review did not search a population death registry or a BDSM-participant cohort. (Methods; Results; Discussion—BDSM activities)

“Consensual” in this setting is an inclusion description inherited from retrospective reports. A deceased person cannot be interviewed, and forensic records may have incomplete or disputed evidence about negotiation, intent, and what happened. The review itself discusses scene alteration, witness stress, stigma, and inconsistent statements as reconstruction problems. Therefore, inclusion in the review should not be treated as independent proof that every act in every case remained consensual. (Discussion—Causes of death)

## Search and classification method

The authors searched PubMed, Embase through OvidSP, and Medline through OvidSP for German- and English-language articles. Their query combined BDSM, sadism, masochism, erotic death, asphyxiophilia, sexual asphyxia, kink, and death concepts. Reports limited to autoerotic death were excluded. To make differently described asphyxia cases more comparable, the authors adopted a published forensic classification. (Methods)

The report does not provide several elements expected from a modern systematic review:

- a search date or complete database-specific search strategy;
- a registered protocol;
- duplicate independent screening or extraction;
- a flow diagram showing records found, deduplicated, excluded, and retained;
- a formal appraisal of case-report quality or risk of bias;
- a comprehensive grey-literature, registry, or non-German/non-English search;
- a method for detecting duplicate publication of the same incident. (Methods; Strengths and limitations)

source_label: How safe is BDSM? A literature review on fatal outcome in BDSM play
text:
The appropriate label is therefore a literature review and case compilation, not a complete census. Failure to find a case is not evidence that it did not occur.

## Evidence base and denominators

The review found 14 case reports and one study published between 1986 and 2020. Across those publications, the authors tabulated 17 deaths. One included study was a 25-year medicolegal postmortem series in Germany covering 1993–2017. That study contained 16,437 autopsies, including 74 non-natural deaths associated with sexual activity; three of those 74 were categorized as BDSM-associated. (Results)

Those numbers have three distinct denominators:

| Reported quantity | Numerator | Denominator | What it describes |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Cases in the literature review | 17 | no exposed population denominator | cases located in selected publications |
| BDSM-associated cases within the German sexual-death subset | 3 | 74 | share of non-natural sexual-activity deaths in one postmortem series |
| BDSM-associated cases within all autopsies in that series | 3 | 16,437 | share of autopsies in that institution or study frame |

The second ratio is about a selected set of non-natural sexual-activity deaths. The third is about autopsies. Neither is the risk per BDSM practitioner or per scene, and neither supplies a general-population incidence rate. The 14 standalone case reports supply no denominator at all. (Results; Discussion—BDSM activities)

The paper describes the fatal outcome as rare and compares the selected BDSM reports with autoerotic and natural sexual-activity deaths. Such comparisons should be treated cautiously. The underlying studies use different case definitions, jurisdictions, periods, ascertainment systems, and denominators. A count of published case reports cannot establish that one activity is more or less common than another. (Discussion—BDSM activities; Strengths and limitations)

## Aggregate findings, with missingness attached

The following counts describe only the 17 published cases collected by the review:

- The review categorized 15 of 17 deaths as strangulation, or 88.2 percent of its case set.
- The two remaining reported causes were one suffocation or oronasal-occlusion case and one severe-hemorrhage case in which alcohol intoxication was also reported.
- A toxicology result for the deceased was mentioned in 13 of 17 cases. Eight of those 13 were reported positive, and five negative.
- In four cases, the partner reportedly tested positive for the same substance as the deceased.
- Experience information was sufficiently described for both participants in nine cases; the review characterized both participants in those nine as not new to BDSM.
- Safeword use was almost entirely missing: one case explicitly noted no safeword, while the other 16 did not report the variable. (Abstract; Results—Experience; Cause of death; Toxicological examinations; Discussion—BDSM communities and safeguards)

The source also states that drugs or alcohol were involved in 64.3 percent of fatal BDSM play. That percentage does not use all 17 cases as its denominator, because 64.3 percent corresponds to 9 of 14 rather than a fraction of 17. The text appears to count nine cases with a positive finding for at least one participant among 14 cases with some toxicology or intoxication information, but it does not clearly define that denominator. This digest retains the underlying reported counts and treats the 64.3-percent statement as denominator-ambiguous. (Abstract; Results—Toxicological examinations; source case table)

Aggregated age and sex descriptions do not solve the denominator problem. They characterize the published cases, not who practices BDSM or who is at risk. Small selected case sets should not be used to profile a “typical” victim or participant.

## Forensic category distinctions

The review separates four broad asphyxia categories: strangulation, suffocation, mechanical asphyxia, and drowning. Within its scheme:

- **Strangulation** is external neck compression and includes manual strangulation, ligature strangulation, and hanging.
- **Suffocation** covers mechanisms that obstruct respiratory openings or airways or deprive a person of an adequate atmosphere; the source distinguishes smothering from choking.
- **Mechanical asphyxia** is presented as an umbrella for position-related or external-compression mechanisms that prevent effective breathing.
- **Drowning** is treated separately as respiratory impairment associated with immersion. (Methods; source Table 1)

source_label: How safe is BDSM? A literature review on fatal outcome in BDSM play
text:
This vocabulary matters because colloquial “choking” is often used for external neck compression. In the review's forensic scheme, external pressure on the neck is strangulation; choking refers to blockage below the epiglottis. Using the mechanism rather than a scene label reduces ambiguity. (Discussion—Causes of death)

The source table contains a likely internal label inversion. Its row labeled “traumatic asphyxia” describes inability to breathe because of body position, while its row labeled “positional asphyxia” describes external chest compression by a heavy object. This digest does not silently teach either row as authoritative. A forensic or clinical answer should verify current specialist definitions rather than rely on that table alone.

Category discipline is also necessary at a higher level. Partnered BDSM fatalities, autoerotic fatalities, homicide, suicide, natural deaths during sexual activity, and deaths whose manner remains uncertain are not interchangeable categories. Scene appearance alone may not determine intent, consent, or manner of death. (Methods; Discussion—Causes of death)

## What the selected cause distribution does and does not show

Fifteen strangulation cases among 17 published cases show that lethal neck-compression events were prominent in the literature the authors located. The result is directly relevant to warnings about breath control and neck compression. It remains a distribution within a publication-derived case set. (Results—Cause of death; Discussion—Causes of death)

It does not show that:

- the selected 88.2-percent figure generalizes beyond this review's published cases;
- fatal and nonfatal BDSM incidents have the same cause distribution;
- rope bondage generally produces the same risk as neck compression;
- a nonfatal history establishes a safe threshold;
- a particular setup can be made safe by reproducing or modifying a reported case;
- experience, instruction, an observer, a safeword, or resuscitation knowledge removes the hazard.

Publication bias is especially plausible for unusual, forensically complex, or sensational mechanisms. Common but nonfatal injuries are outside the review by design. Fatal events that were not recognized, reported, published, indexed, or written in German or English could be absent. Therefore, the observed 15-of-17 concentration may reflect both genuine hazard and the way cases reach journals. (Methods; Strengths and limitations)

## Toxicology: association is not causation

Eight positive results among the 13 deceased persons with reported toxicology show that substances were detected in a majority of that documented subset. The case compilation does not include an uninjured or nonfatal comparison group, consistent testing panels, concentrations for every finding, standardized impairment assessment, or a denominator for substance use across BDSM activity. Missing toxicology is not equivalent to a negative result. Detection is not automatically proof of impairment, contribution, or causation. (Results—Toxicological examinations; Discussion—Toxicology)

The review's conclusion says alcohol or drug use “correlates” with fatalities. A set containing only fatalities cannot estimate a correlation between exposure and fatal outcome: it has no outcome-negative comparison group. At most, the paper documents co-occurrence in selected fatal reports. It is reasonable safety practice to avoid intoxicants when judgment, communication, motor control, monitoring, or emergency response matter, but the review does not quantify how much a substance changes fatality risk. (Discussion—Toxicology; Conclusion)

Some toxicology labels in the case source material are vague, and the paper acknowledges that particular labels were not clarified. Combining different substances, doses, tests, and partner findings into one percentage further limits interpretation. Answers derived from this source should describe reported positive toxicology in selected cases without assigning a causal percentage.

## Experience, knowledge, and safeguards

The review says both participants were not new to BDSM in all nine cases where experience for both was described. That observation argues against treating familiarity as immunity. It does not show whether participants were competent in the specific activity, whether their information was accurate, or whether a safety practice was followed. “Experienced” was inherited from heterogeneous case reports rather than measured with a common scale. (Results—Experience in BDSM)

The conclusion also associates “lack of knowledge” with fatalities, but the compiled data do not support that association. Knowledge was not measured consistently; experience was missing or partial in eight cases; and the nine cases with information involved people described as non-novices. There was no comparison group of nonfatal scenes. The defensible finding is that prior exposure or instruction did not guarantee prevention in the published cases. (Results—Experience in BDSM; Conclusion)

The article describes community education, mentoring, negotiation, first aid, safewords, traffic-light communication, emergency planning, and rapid-release readiness as safeguards discussed in BDSM communities. These are contextual recommendations, not interventions evaluated by the review. The case series cannot estimate their effectiveness. (Discussion—BDSM communities and safeguards)

source_label: How safe is BDSM? A literature review on fatal outcome in BDSM play
text:
Safeword data are a particularly clear example of missingness. One reported absence and 16 unreported cases cannot establish an association in either direction. A safeword is a communication tool, not proof that a high-risk mechanism is safe, and communication can fail when consciousness, speech, breathing, or motor control is impaired.

The article is internally inconsistent about pre-event instruction and resuscitation discussion. The results section describes two cases in which partners were instructed about breath control and taught cardiopulmonary resuscitation; the safeguards discussion refers to three cases in which instruction and resuscitation were discussed. Without a reconciled extraction table, this digest does not choose one count. It records the discrepancy and retains only the broader lesson that preparation and attempted resuscitation did not prevent every reported death. (Results—Experience in BDSM; Discussion—BDSM communities and safeguards)

## Forensic reconstruction and stigma

The review notes that post-event reconstruction can be distorted by emergency intervention, scene changes, fear, shame, witness trauma, or incomplete statements. Stigma may also discourage disclosure and medical help. These points justify careful, nonjudgmental fact gathering. They do not justify assuming that a scene was altered or that a surviving partner is deceptive. (Discussion—Causes of death; Strengths and limitations)

For corpus use, the transferable principles are:

- describe observed mechanism and evidence separately from inferred intent;
- distinguish what a report documented from what it omitted;
- preserve uncertainty about consent, chronology, and manner of death;
- avoid stigmatizing BDSM interest as itself diagnostic of a cause or intent;
- avoid treating community terminology as a substitute for forensic terminology;
- never reconstruct a dangerous technique as an instructional sequence.

The paper suggests that forensic investigators may seek contextual materials to understand an event. This digest omits its operational reconstruction detail because it is outside rope education and could encourage intrusive or sensational treatment of a death.

## Strong conclusions and overclaims

The source supports these bounded statements:

- The authors found 17 published partnered BDSM-associated fatal cases under their search and language criteria.
- Fifteen were categorized as strangulation under the review's chosen forensic scheme.
- Toxicology, experience, and safeword reporting were incomplete.
- Some fatal reports involved participants described as experienced.
- The paper documents the need for precise mechanism language and emergency readiness.
- The evidence base is small and shaped by publication and ascertainment. (Methods; Results; Strengths and limitations)

The source does not establish these stronger claims:

- a population incidence or per-scene fatality rate;
- that BDSM fatalities are rarer than another class under comparable denominators;
- that alcohol, drugs, inexperience, or absent safeguards caused the selected deaths as a group;
- that all 17 cases were independently verified as consensual throughout;
- that any safeguard makes breath control or neck compression safe;
- universal preventability;
- that the selected cases represent rope bondage as a whole.

The abstract and conclusion use preventability and correlation language more strongly than the review design warrants. A safety-oriented model should preserve the authors' recommendations while declining to present those causal claims as measured results.

## Practical evidence discipline

When this paper is used to answer a safety question:

1. Name it as a literature review of published fatal cases.
2. State the 17-case publication-derived denominator.
3. Attach missingness to every subgroup count.
4. Keep partnered BDSM, autoerotic activity, natural death, and broader sexual-activity death categories distinct.
5. Use mechanism terms such as external neck compression rather than ambiguous scene slang.
6. Treat the 15-of-17 result as a signal about selected fatal reports, not a prevalence estimate.
7. Describe toxicology as detection or reported involvement, not proven impairment or cause.
8. Say that experience and community safeguards did not eliminate risk; do not estimate their protective effect from this review.
9. Do not reproduce a fatal configuration or turn a case into a how-to example.
10. Emphasize that breath control and neck compression retain potentially fatal risk and are not made safe by a numerical threshold, safeword, observer, or prior success.

## Source-section disposition map

source_label: How safe is BDSM? A literature review on fatal outcome in BDSM play
text:
| Source region | Corpus treatment |
|---|---|
| Abstract | Findings retained with denominator, missingness, and overclaim corrections |
| Introduction | Research question and partnered-BDSM scope retained; prevalence claims excluded |
| Materials and methods | Databases, language, search concepts, autoerotic exclusion, and classification approach retained |
| Case table | Individual rows and technique descriptions excluded; only auditable aggregates retained |
| Results | Counts, denominators, missingness, and category distribution retained |
| Discussion of activities | Only non-instructional risk boundaries retained |
| Causes-of-death discussion | Mechanism terminology and reconstruction uncertainty retained; operational scene detail excluded |
| Toxicology discussion | Counts retained; correlation claim explicitly downgraded |
| Communities and safeguards | Education and emergency-readiness context retained; efficacy claims not inferred |
| Strengths and limitations | Expanded with selection, publication, language, and denominator limitations |
| Conclusion | Bounded findings retained; prevalence, causal-correlation, and universal-prevention language rejected |
| References and associated data | Excluded from training text; article identifiers remain in attribution and manifest |

This digest should be sampled as one source document. Any later QA must inherit the same split assignment and should test interpretation of methods, denominators, missingness, or safety limits—not title, author, DOI, PMCID, PMID, page, or URL recall.

source_label: Entrapment neuropathies: a contemporary approach to pathophysiology, clinical assessment, and management
text:
# Entrapment neuropathy: clinical distinctions without a rope threshold

Source: Annina B. Schmid, Joel Fundaun, and Brigitte Tampin, “Entrapment neuropathies: a contemporary approach to pathophysiology, clinical assessment, and management,” *Pain Reports* 5, e829, published 22 July 2020, DOI 10.1097/PR9.0000000000000829, PMCID PMC7382548, PMID 32766466.

License and adaptation notice: the article is CC BY 4.0. This manually curated adaptation narrows the review to pathophysiology, presentation, assessment, and uncertainty. It separates human evidence from animal and other preclinical evidence, omits figures and an image-based table, and deliberately excludes prognosis and management detail.

## Clinical and rope-transfer boundary

This is a clinical narrative review of entrapment neuropathies such as carpal tunnel syndrome and nerve-root conditions grouped under “sciatica.” It does not study rope bondage, acute rope compression, suspension, pressure from a wrap, loading direction, contact width, placement, duration, or release. It supplies no safe rope pressure, no safe body location, no safe exposure time, no schedule for checking a tied person, and no waiting period after symptoms. It cannot be used to calculate when a nerve will be injured or when a scene may continue. (Schmid et al., 2020, §§1–4)

The article's management and natural-history sections address diagnosed clinical populations, chiefly carpal tunnel syndrome and heterogeneous “sciatica” cohorts. Those data do not determine recovery, prognosis, treatment, self-care, release timing, or return to rope after a rope-related symptom. This digest does not reproduce those recommendations or outcome percentages. (Schmid et al., 2020, §5)

Numbness, tingling, altered sensation, electric or burning pain, weakness, loss of coordination, or other neurological symptoms after rope require evaluation by a qualified medical professional. A symptom report is not a diagnosis. Do not use this review to self-diagnose an entrapment neuropathy, choose a treatment, or decide that a symptom is harmless because it improved. Persistent, progressive, recurrent, or motor symptoms increase the importance of timely professional assessment; the article does not define a safe delay.

## What kind of review this is

The paper is a contemporary narrative review. It synthesizes mechanisms, clinical presentation, assessment, and management across multiple conditions. It does not report a systematic search strategy, prespecified eligibility criteria, duplicate screening, a flow diagram, or a uniform risk-of-bias appraisal. Its cited evidence includes animal compression models, cellular and imaging studies, observational human studies, diagnostic studies, guidelines, trials, and other reviews. Conclusions must retain the design of the evidence that supports them. (Schmid et al., 2020, §§1–6)

The authors emphasize heterogeneity. “Entrapment neuropathy” is not one uniform biological state. Different people with the same diagnostic label may have different mixtures of ischemia, edema, demyelination, axonal injury, inflammation, mechanosensitivity, central nervous system adaptation, contextual factors, and comorbidities. Conversely, similar symptoms can arise from different disorders. (Schmid et al., 2020, Key Points; §§2–4)

This heterogeneity is a reason for clinical assessment, not a menu for self-classification. A person cannot infer which mechanism is present from one sensation, one body location, one provocation maneuver, or one image.

## Terms that should not be collapsed

**Peripheral nerve entrapment** refers to compression or irritation of a peripheral nerve as it passes through a constrained anatomical space. The review's main distal example is carpal tunnel syndrome; it also discusses cubital tunnel syndrome. These are established clinical entities with anatomical, historical, examination, and sometimes electrodiagnostic or imaging context. (Schmid et al., 2020, §1)

**“Sciatica”** is an imprecise umbrella term. In different studies it can include somatic referred pain, radicular pain, and radiculopathy. Mixing those categories helps explain the very wide range of reported prevalence and treatment effects. The review uses more specific language when an underlying study defines its population. (Schmid et al., 2020, §1)

**Radicular pain** is pain associated with abnormal activity arising from a spinal nerve root or its ganglion. It can occur without demonstrable loss of nerve-root function. (Schmid et al., 2020, §§1, 3)

source_label: Entrapment neuropathies: a contemporary approach to pathophysiology, clinical assessment, and management
text:
**Radiculopathy** is a lesion or conduction disturbance affecting a spinal nerve or root that manifests with loss of function, such as sensory deficit in an anatomical distribution, muscle weakness in a myotomal pattern, or altered reflexes. Pain may coexist, but pain alone is not the defining loss-of-function sign. (Schmid et al., 2020, §§1, 3)

**Neuropathic pain** and **nerve damage** are related but not interchangeable. A person may have nerve-related pain without obvious loss of function, while a nerve lesion may produce numbness or weakness without prominent pain. Screening-questionnaire categories are not a substitute for neurological examination. (Schmid et al., 2020, §§3, 4.1)

**Neural mechanosensitivity** means that mechanical provocation of neural tissue produces an abnormal pain response. It can occur without a demonstrable nerve lesion, and a confirmed entrapment neuropathy can exist without heightened mechanosensitivity. It is therefore neither necessary nor sufficient for diagnosis. (Schmid et al., 2020, §§3, 4.2.2)

An acute symptom following external rope pressure is not automatically a chronic anatomical entrapment syndrome. This review cannot determine whether such a symptom represents transient conduction disturbance, axonal injury, inflammation, musculoskeletal referral, a spinal source, another neurological disorder, or something else.

## Evidence ladder: human findings versus preclinical mechanisms

Much mechanistic knowledge about neuropathic pain comes from animal models of acute, severe nerve injury. The authors warn that common human entrapment neuropathies often develop slowly and involve milder, chronic compromise. They discuss refined animal models intended to resemble human entrapment more closely, but a closer model remains a model. It does not establish a safe exposure for a person. (Schmid et al., 2020, §2)

A disciplined reading labels each claim:

- **Preclinical observation:** measured in an animal, tissue, or experimental model.
- **Human association:** observed in a clinical sample without necessarily proving cause.
- **Human diagnostic-performance finding:** estimates how a test behaved in a defined population.
- **Clinical inference:** a proposed explanation connecting mechanism to presentation.
- **Guideline or management recommendation:** depends on a diagnosed condition and a professional decision context.

The source often moves from preclinical biology to a possible clinical explanation using terms such as “may,” “could,” or “is thought to.” Those transitions should remain hypotheses, not rope-design rules.

## Ischemia, edema, and fibrosis

The review describes reduced intraneural blood flow as one feature of mild entrapment neuropathy. Experimental compression studies support a pressure-sensitive venous circulation, while human clinical studies report nerve enlargement and altered imaging signal consistent with edema in some entrapment conditions. Persistent edema is proposed as a contributor to internal and external fibrotic change, which may reduce normal nerve movement. (Schmid et al., 2020, §2.1)

The source includes experimental pressure values. They are deliberately omitted here. An experimental pressure at which one physiological change was observed is not a no-injury threshold. It cannot be transferred across species, tissues, measurement methods, pressure distributions, nerve locations, individual anatomy, repeated loading, shear, stretch, ischemia, or rope contact geometry. It is especially not a target value for bondage equipment.

Intermittent symptoms that vary with position or movement are discussed as potentially compatible with changes in intraneural circulation. That proposed mechanism is not a home test. Symptom improvement with movement does not establish that the nerve is undamaged or that exposure can resume. (Schmid et al., 2020, §2.1)

## Demyelination and axonal injury

Focal demyelination is common in established entrapment neuropathies and can contribute to slowed or blocked conduction. The review also notes that ischemia may alter conduction without demyelination. With persistent compression or irritation, axons may degenerate. These are different biological processes with different clinical and test manifestations. (Schmid et al., 2020, §2.2)

Large myelinated fibers contribute to light-touch sensation and motor function. Their dysfunction can appear as numbness, weakness, muscle wasting, or reflex change. The paper also reviews evidence that small-fiber structure and function can be affected and may sometimes change before conventional large-fiber findings. Small fibers contribute to thermal and pain sensation, so a normal coarse-touch check cannot exclude all nerve involvement. (Schmid et al., 2020, §§2.2, 4.2.1)

source_label: Entrapment neuropathies: a contemporary approach to pathophysiology, clinical assessment, and management
text:
The source does not give a symptom sequence that reliably grades injury. The absence of pain does not rule out loss of function; the presence of pain does not prove axonal damage; and symptom resolution does not measure remyelination or axonal recovery.

## Inflammation and axonal transport

The strongest evidence linking neuroinflammation to neuropathic pain comes from preclinical models, particularly acute or severe injuries. The review also identifies growing experimental evidence in milder chronic compression and some human imaging evidence of local or remote inflammatory activity in lumbar radicular pain. Immune signaling may sensitize injured and uninjured fibers and may help explain pain beyond a single textbook nerve territory. (Schmid et al., 2020, §2.3)

Compression and inflammation can impair transport of materials along an axon in experimental work. The authors propose that transport disruption and changes in ion-channel distribution may contribute to mechanosensitivity and abnormal activity. This is mechanistic reasoning, not a clinically measurable rope threshold. (Schmid et al., 2020, §2.4)

The paper also describes inflammation around a nerve as a possible source of pain even without clear axonal damage. That distinction helps explain why pain, structural nerve damage, and abnormal conduction do not always travel together. It does not make pain without measurable damage unimportant.

## Central nervous system and psychosocial contributions

Peripheral nerve injury can be accompanied by changes in spinal and brain processing. The review cites clinical findings such as bilateral sensory differences in some unilateral conditions, widespread hypersensitivity, altered pain modulation, and cortical changes. Whether central changes remain dependent on peripheral input or can maintain symptoms independently is unresolved. (Schmid et al., 2020, §2.5)

These observations should not be used to tell a person that symptoms are “just central,” “just sensitization,” or imagined. Nor do widespread symptoms prove a central mechanism. The review presents a debated, mixed biological system in which peripheral and central processes can interact.

Evidence for psychosocial factors as causes or prognostic factors is limited or inconsistent in the entrapment conditions reviewed. At the same time, persistent nerve-related pain can have substantial psychological and social consequences. Consequence, context, and cause should not be conflated. (Schmid et al., 2020, §2.6)

## Symptoms, signs, and diagnosis

A **symptom** is experienced and reported by the person. The review describes possible symptoms including tingling or other paresthesia, numbness, electric-shock-like pain, spontaneous pain attacks, and pain provoked by movement or mechanical contact. Distribution may extend beyond a classic dermatome or named peripheral-nerve territory. (Schmid et al., 2020, §3)

A **sign** is observed or elicited in examination. Loss-of-function signs may include reduced sensation, weakness, atrophy, or altered reflexes. Gain-of-function signs can include increased pain from normally painful stimuli, pain from normally nonpainful stimuli, or abnormal responses during controlled neural provocation. (Schmid et al., 2020, §§3, 4.2.1)

A **diagnosis** integrates history, progression, distribution, examination, alternative explanations, and, when appropriate, investigations. One symptom does not identify a lesion. One normal sign does not exclude it. One positive maneuver does not prove it. One imaging finding does not establish that it causes the symptoms. (Schmid et al., 2020, §§3–4.2.3)

Nerve compression on imaging can be asymptomatic. Conversely, clinically important symptoms may exist when a study is negative. The review reports that pain outside a canonical anatomical territory is common in its example conditions. Pattern mismatch should neither automatically exclude nerve involvement nor be used to diagnose it. (Schmid et al., 2020, §3)

## Clinical assessment

The review describes assessment as a detailed history plus physical examination, followed by additional testing when indicated. History includes symptom location, distribution, quality, intensity, daily behavior, progression, potential mechanism, health conditions, and occupational or contextual factors. Clinicians also consider differential diagnoses and serious disorders that can resemble an entrapment neuropathy. (Schmid et al., 2020, §4.2)

Neurological examination includes motor function, reflexes, muscle strength, and sensory function. Because large and small fibers may both be involved, a complete examination is broader than asking whether light touch feels equal. Clinical testing requires training, controlled technique, appropriate comparison, and interpretation in the whole presentation. (Schmid et al., 2020, §4.2.1)

source_label: Entrapment neuropathies: a contemporary approach to pathophysiology, clinical assessment, and management
text:
The article's image-based differential-diagnosis table is excluded from this digest. It is a clinical decision aid, not a rope-screening checklist. A lay checklist could falsely reassure someone whose presentation is incomplete, evolving, or caused by a different disorder.

## Provocation tests are not binary truth machines

The review discusses positional or neural provocation maneuvers used in carpal tunnel syndrome, cervical radicular presentations, and lower-limb nerve-related pain. Reported diagnostic performance varies substantially between studies. An abnormal response is more than discomfort during a stretch; clinical interpretation considers whether the person's symptoms are reproduced and whether a remote structural change alters the response. (Schmid et al., 2020, §4.2.2)

Even when performed correctly, heightened mechanosensitivity can occur without a nerve lesion, and a confirmed lesion may lack mechanosensitivity. Self-performing a named maneuver after rope exposure cannot clear the nerve, locate an injury, or determine whether rope can resume. This digest omits maneuver instructions to avoid converting a clinical examination into self-triage.

## Imaging and nerve-conduction limitations

Nerve-conduction studies can be particularly useful for distal entrapment neuropathies, but their value is less established for some proximal conditions. The review notes that early carpal tunnel involvement may escape detection. A normal study therefore does not have one universal meaning. (Schmid et al., 2020, §4.2.3)

Magnetic resonance imaging is commonly used for proximal conditions, yet false positives and false negatives occur, and the level suggested by imaging may not match the clinical examination. Ultrasound can show nerve enlargement in some distal entrapments and may be convenient for side-to-side comparison, but size is one finding within a diagnostic workup. (Schmid et al., 2020, §4.2.3)

Investigations can support diagnosis, distinguish alternatives, or identify serious pathology. They do not replace history and examination. Test performance depends on the condition, stage, reference standard, population, protocol, and interpreter.

The neuropathic-pain grading framework also has limitations in entrapment neuropathy. Symptoms may spread beyond the expected territory, sensory loss may be subtle, bedside tests may miss it, gain-of-function symptoms may dominate, and a confirmatory test may be negative or not indicated. The paper therefore describes unresolved classification problems rather than a simple scoring solution. (Schmid et al., 2020, §4.1)

## Rope-related use: what can be said

This review supports a modest set of rope-relevant clinical principles:

- Neurological symptoms and objective nerve dysfunction are not the same thing.
- Pain, numbness, weakness, sensory change, and mechanosensitivity can occur in different combinations.
- A symptom's location may not map neatly to one textbook nerve territory.
- Improvement, a normal touch comparison, a negative self-provocation maneuver, or a prior uneventful exposure does not prove absence of injury.
- A single positive maneuver, scan, or electrical study does not make a diagnosis by itself.
- Human presentation is heterogeneous, and animal compression findings cannot define safe rope practice.
- New neurological symptoms after rope deserve qualified medical evaluation rather than a pressure calculation or online self-treatment plan.

The review does not support:

- converting an experimental pressure into a rope limit;
- ranking body locations as safe or unsafe;
- specifying wrap width, tension, direction, duration, or check interval;
- teaching a release maneuver or deciding whether release was fast enough;
- predicting recovery from the speed or completeness of symptom improvement;
- prescribing rest, heat, cold, massage, movement, splinting, medication, or exercise;
- deciding when someone can return to rope;
- treating a diagnosed carpal tunnel or radicular cohort as evidence about acute bondage compression.

Questions about treatment, prognosis, urgent triage, or return to activity require current clinical guidance and an individual medical assessment, not extrapolation from this narrative review.

## Evidence and rights disposition

The article's four figures were not fetched or retained. One figure is explicitly adapted from another publication, and the article's table is stored as an external image rather than structured JATS text. None of those visual components is part of the training artifact. Captions are used only to understand scope.

The pathophysiology sections are retained as a source-attributed synthesis with evidence labels. The clinical-presentation and assessment sections are retained with test limitations. Numerical experimental pressures, prevalence, natural-history percentages, treatment effects, medication claims, surgical outcomes, and management instructions are excluded because they do not answer the bounded corpus question and are especially prone to unsafe rope transfer.

## Compact interpretation discipline

When using this source:

source_label: Entrapment neuropathies: a contemporary approach to pathophysiology, clinical assessment, and management
text:
1. Identify whether a statement comes from an animal model, a human association, a diagnostic study, a guideline, or a hypothesis.
2. Keep symptom, examination sign, test result, and diagnosis separate.
3. Preserve the distinction between radicular pain and radiculopathy.
4. Treat anatomical distribution as evidence, not a perfect rule.
5. Attach false-positive, false-negative, stage, and population limitations to tests.
6. Do not use mechanosensitivity as proof for or against nerve injury.
7. Do not transfer experimental magnitude or duration to rope.
8. Do not derive treatment, recovery, prognosis, or return-to-rope advice.
9. Direct rope-related neurological symptoms to qualified medical evaluation.

## Source-section map

| Source region | Training treatment |
|---|---|
| Key points and introduction | Review scope and heterogeneity retained; prevalence values excluded |
| Pathophysiology overview | Human-versus-preclinical distinction retained |
| Ischemia, edema, fibrosis | Mechanisms retained; experimental pressure numbers excluded |
| Demyelination and axon degeneration | Large/small fiber and loss-of-function distinctions retained |
| Neuroinflammation and axonal transport | Evidence hierarchy and proposed mechanisms retained |
| Central and psychosocial contributions | Uncertainty and non-stigmatizing interpretation retained |
| Clinical presentation | Symptom, sign, distribution, and damage distinctions retained |
| Neuropathic pain grading | Classification limitations retained; treatment trigger excluded |
| Clinical and neurological assessment | Professional assessment scope retained; figure and self-testing instructions excluded |
| Mechanosensitivity | Diagnostic limitations retained; maneuver instructions excluded |
| Imaging and nerve conduction | False-positive, false-negative, stage, and location limitations retained |
| Natural history, prognosis, and management | Excluded from training content except to state non-transfer |
| Table and figures | Binary images not fetched; no visual content retained |
| References | Excluded from training content; article identifiers retained for attribution |

All chunks and any later derived QA must remain in the same source-document split. Derived material should test evidence distinctions and uncertainty, never title, author, DOI, PMCID, PMID, page, URL, pressure, duration, placement, treatment, or prognosis recall.

source_label: Suspension syndrome: a scoping review and ICAR MEDCOM recommendations
text:
# Suspension syndrome — ICAR scoping-review evidence corpus

Captured: 2026-07-16T08:40:57Z

Source article: Suspension syndrome: a scoping review and recommendations from the International Commission for Mountain Emergency Medicine (ICAR MEDCOM)

Authors: Simon Rauch, Raimund Lechner, Giacomo Strapazzon, Roger B Mortimer, John Ellerton, Sven Christjar Skaiaa, Tobias Huber, Hermann Brugger, Mathieu Pasquier, Peter Paal

Journal record: Scandinavian Journal of Trauma, Resuscitation and Emergency Medicine 31, article 95 (9 December 2023)

Identifiers: DOI 10.1186/s13049-023-01164-z; PMCID PMC10710713; PMID 38071341

Authorized source route: https://www.ebi.ac.uk/europepmc/webservices/rest/PMC10710713/fullTextXML

License: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

This non-Q&A document is an original, attributed evidence summary of the captured JATS article. It preserves review methods, denominators, source grades, consensus recommendations, contradictions, and limitations. It does not provide a bondage suspension method; harness-rescue evidence is not validated for bondage suspension.

## Source scope and transfer boundary

Categories: anatomy_injury_prevention, emergency_procedures, teaching_evaluation

Rauch and colleagues reviewed suspension syndrome in occupational rope access, climbing, mountain rescue, experimental harness suspension, and reported accidents, then developed International Commission for Alpine Rescue Medical Commission recommendations. The source defines the syndrome around passive hanging in a vertical or near-vertical position, usually in a harness. Its evidence is not a study of rope bondage, decorative rope, bondage-specific load paths, or bondage participants. Any relevance to bondage suspension is therefore a cautious hazard analogy, not validated transfer of incidence, equipment findings, rescue technique, or treatment thresholds.

## Review question and two-part evidence process

Categories: teaching_evaluation

The work has two distinct components. First, it is a scoping review seeking original epidemiological or medical data about suspension-syndrome mechanisms. Second, the author group drafted recommendations from the review, prior recommendations, discussion within ICAR MEDCOM, review by all four ICAR commissions at the 2019 meeting, and final circulation through the ICAR MEDCOM list server. Consensus recommendations should not be mistaken for effects directly tested in controlled treatment trials.

## Search, eligibility, extraction, and appraisal

Categories: teaching_evaluation

The authors searched PubMed, Embase, Web of Science, and the Cochrane Library through 17 September 2023 using seven suspension-syndrome and harness-hanging terms joined with OR. They also screened bibliographies and personal article databases. Eligible material needed original epidemiological or pathophysiological data such as vital signs, laboratory values, or objective symptoms. They excluded duplicates, unrelated topics, unavailable full text, registrations, abstracts, reviews without original data, case reports without original data, letters, editorials, short communications, and languages outside the authors' English, German, or Italian. Two authors independently extracted and appraised data with National Heart, Lung and Blood Institute study-quality tools, resolving disagreements by discussion; the authors report no inter-rater reliability calculation.

## Included evidence and quality profile

Categories: teaching_evaluation

The body reports that 121 records were returned, 70 remained after title and abstract screening, 47 were excluded at full text, and 23 articles were included. Of those 23, two supplied epidemiological survey data and 21 addressed pathophysiology. Designs ranged from autopsy reports and case reports or series to manikin work and small observational or interventional human physiology studies. From the review's own table, 9 studies were rated good, 8 fair, and 6 poor. No randomized controlled interventional study was found. The review also found no experimental studies directly testing prevention, diagnosis, or treatment.

## Epidemiology remains uncertain

Categories: anatomy_injury_prevention, teaching_evaluation

The review calls suspension syndrome rare but says exact epidemiology is unavailable. One 2002 enquiry reported no suspension syndrome during 5.8 million on-rope hours among technicians qualified by the International Rope Access Trade Association. A small survey of height-rescue organizations identified three cases but did not provide an observation period or adequate symptom and severity detail. These observations come from trained industrial or rescue systems and cannot be converted into a rate for climbing generally, for other harness systems, or for bondage suspension. Absence of reported events is not proof of zero risk.

## Venous pooling is observed, but the traditional collapse story is unproven

source_label: Suspension syndrome: a scoping review and ICAR MEDCOM recommendations
text:
Categories: anatomy_injury_prevention, teaching_evaluation

Experimental studies show venous pooling in the legs during motionless harness suspension. The review nevertheless finds no proof that pooling alone produces a progressive loss of cardiac preload that directly causes cardiac arrest. In the cited experiments, pooling was not accompanied by the expected major systemic haemodynamic deterioration before presyncope; mild tachycardia and hypertension were sometimes attributed to discomfort and sympathetic activation. The authors therefore reject a simple proven blood-pooling-to-arrest chain while retaining an uncertain contributory role for orthostatic stress.

## Neurocardiogenic mechanism is favored, not fully resolved

Categories: anatomy_injury_prevention, teaching_evaluation

More recent experimental evidence favored a sudden neurocardiogenic response: heart rate, blood pressure, and stroke volume dropped around presyncope, with reduced cerebral oxygenation and a pattern resembling vasodilatory and cardio-inhibitory syncope. The exact trigger remains unknown. The review discusses pain, altered lower-limb sensation, inability to move, and orthostatic stress as possible contributors. It notes that proposed baroreceptor and Bezold–Jarisch explanations were not demonstrated in the cited experiments. This is a favored mechanism assembled from small physiology studies, not a complete causal model of every collapse during suspension.

## Symptoms and timing are variable and sometimes abrupt

Categories: anatomy_injury_prevention, emergency_procedures

Reported presyncopal features include light-headedness, dizziness, confusion, pallor, cold sweating, warmth or hot flashes, blurred vision, nausea, and bradycardia. Experimental tolerance varied markedly between people: symptoms could begin within minutes or much later, and one study found abrupt presyncope without a warning phase. Increased body weight and prior exercise were associated with shorter tolerance in some studies, while sex was not. These heterogeneous observations do not provide a safe waiting time, a reliable warning sequence, or a person-level prediction.

## Why loss of consciousness while still suspended is dangerous

Categories: anatomy_injury_prevention, emergency_procedures

Ordinary fainting allows a fall toward horizontal, which can restore cerebral perfusion. A person who loses consciousness while attached in a near-vertical suspension may remain upright and cannot make that compensatory position change. The review also identifies possible airway obstruction from neck flexion or extension. With prolonged suspension, reported concerns include tissue hypoperfusion, rhabdomyolysis, hyperkalaemia, acute kidney injury, hypothermia, thromboembolic events, and neurological compression injury. Evidence for these later stages is sparse because human experiments ethically stop at early symptoms.

## Harness and attachment findings are context-specific

Categories: anatomy_injury_prevention, uplines_suspension_hardpoints, teaching_evaluation

Older belt-only and chest-harness systems were associated with rapid pain, breathing restriction, and nerve pressure and are treated by the review as obsolete relative to modern sit harnesses. Some studies found lower tolerance with dorsal industrial attachment, while findings were not uniform. Fit, sizing, clothing, body dimensions, attachment position, and hanging angle can affect comfort or tolerance. The authors explicitly warn that anthropometry and tolerance from industrial dorsal systems cannot simply be transferred to ventral sit harnesses used in mountain sports. The same restriction applies more strongly to bondage rope, which is not a certified sit harness and was not evaluated in this review.

## Proposed definition and classification

Categories: anatomy_injury_prevention, emergency_procedures, teaching_evaluation

Because terminology was inconsistent, the authors prefer “suspension syndrome” over names that require a harness or imply trauma. Their proposed acute category includes near-suspension syncope, suspension syncope, cardiac arrest while suspended, and cardiac arrest within 60 minutes after rescue. Their proposed subacute category includes lower-limb sensory or motor deficit lasting more than 24 hours, end-organ dysfunction such as rhabdomyolysis-associated acute kidney injury, and cardiac arrest more than 60 minutes after rescue. The framework excludes signs better explained by trauma, hypothermia, hypoglycaemia, or another condition. It is a proposed review classification, not a prospectively validated diagnostic rule.

## ICAR prevention and rescue recommendations, with source grades

Categories: emergency_procedures, uplines_suspension_hardpoints, teaching_evaluation

The article reports seven ICAR recommendations using American College of Chest Physicians grades.
