The categories included apparent external geometry, lay-related geometry, colour intensity, the visible silhouette area, and protruding surface fringes. These are candidate surface descriptors, not validated damage or discard criteria. [INNOTRAC-462-2020, PDF pp. 57 and 60–63]

Industrial inspection-method evidence: One example labelled as a diameter measurement actually used the major axis of an observed ellipse rather than a direct diameter. This distinction matters: an image-derived surrogate can change with rope orientation, flattening, perspective, segmentation, and setup even when the intended physical quantity is unchanged. The paper does not provide a complete uncertainty model for that surrogate. [INNOTRAC-462-2020, PDF pp. 60–61]

Industrial inspection-method evidence: Colour intensity and visible-area measurements are likewise properties of an imaging pipeline as well as of the specimen. The article does not demonstrate invariance across cameras, illumination, exposure, backgrounds, fibre colours, covers, contamination, or independent laboratories. Their trends in project images cannot be treated as universal material-state signals. [INNOTRAC-462-2020, PDF pp. 61–62; whole-article absence audit]

Industrial inspection-method evidence: The paper argues for considering several indicators together because one outward measure may represent only one part of a failure process. Combining indicators can reduce dependence on a single fragile signal, but it also creates a new model that needs calibration, validation, error analysis, and a defined decision context. [INNOTRAC-462-2020, PDF pp. 57–59]

Industrial inspection-method evidence boundary: The article includes an illustrative logical discard example and suggested turning points in plotted project traces. Every rule, threshold, equation-like expression, region, percentage, value, and proposed discard point is excluded. The surviving combination principle cannot be converted into a decision algorithm. [INNOTRAC-462-2020, PDF pp. 58–62]

Industrial inspection-method evidence boundary: Surface indicators can correlate with test progression without identifying a causal damage mechanism. A trend, bend, plateau, or change point in an external metric is not automatically a defect label, a strength estimate, or an end-of-life marker. [INNOTRAC-462-2020, PDF pp. 60–62]

## Project and specimen scope

Industrial inspection-method evidence: The project section concerns high-modulus synthetic fibre specimens in industrial running-rope research. It names braided and laid constructions, including specimens with and without a cover, made from several proprietary synthetic fibre families. This is not a natural-fibre dataset. [INNOTRAC-462-2020, PDF pp. 59–60]

Industrial inspection-method evidence: One research stream used an industrial storage-and-retrieval-machine context, while a student project examined specimens from staged bending tests. Some histories progressed to rupture or near-rupture, whereas the staged work removed specimens at selected points for later imaging. These different histories should not be pooled without preserving their provenance. [INNOTRAC-462-2020, PDF pp. 59–60]

Industrial inspection-method evidence: The project narrative reports repeated image acquisition during test progression, but it does not give a complete sample inventory, allocation method, construction-by-condition matrix, missing-image account, or independent ground-truth protocol. The figures therefore do not define a reusable benchmark dataset. [INNOTRAC-462-2020, PDF pp. 59–62; whole-article absence audit]

Industrial inspection-method evidence: The paper identifies one publicly funded research project and a student project nested within a larger project financed by a German state. It also discloses use of a patented commercial inspection platform, its standard software, and separately programmed processing. These relationships are part of the study context, not endorsements. [INNOTRAC-462-2020, PDF pp. 59–60 and 63]

Industrial inspection-method evidence: No separate conflict-of-interest declaration, formal funding statement, data-availability statement, code release, preregistration, or protocol registration appears in the final article. Funding is described only in the project narrative, and the commercial-platform relationship must therefore remain visible when evaluating the work. [INNOTRAC-462-2020, whole-article disclosure audit, pp. 55–63]

source_label: Camera-based Visual Rope Inspection, innoTRAC Journal (2020)
text:
Industrial inspection-method evidence: The article reports no held-out detection study, sensitivity or specificity, false-positive or false-negative analysis, inter-rater comparison, blinded assessment, independent replication, or external-site validation. It cannot support an inspection-system rating or a claim that one configuration is superior. [INNOTRAC-462-2020, whole-article methods-and-results absence audit, pp. 55–63]

## Surface observability is not internal condition

Industrial inspection-method evidence: Camera images directly observe only exposed surface appearance within their fields of view. They do not directly observe the rope core, buried fibres, internal abrasion, internal heat effects, hidden strand interactions, or other conditions concealed by the construction or cover. [INNOTRAC-462-2020, PDF pp. 55–63; bounded observability inference]

Industrial inspection-method evidence: Multiple surface views can improve external coverage while leaving internal observability unchanged. Combining several surface-derived indicators still produces a surface-evidence model unless independently validated against internal measurements and relevant failure outcomes. The article does not provide that validation. [INNOTRAC-462-2020, PDF pp. 55–63; whole-article validation audit]

Industrial inspection-method evidence: The paper's conclusion expressly characterizes discard criteria for high-modulus fibre ropes as insufficiently researched. That admission governs the more optimistic project language: the work proposes research directions and candidate indicators rather than a reliable finished discard criterion. [INNOTRAC-462-2020, PDF p. 63]

Industrial inspection-method evidence boundary: No image, external measurement, or combined score in this corpus establishes internal condition, residual strength, remaining life, a working load, a safety factor, a discard point, or permission to continue use. [INNOTRAC-462-2020, bounded adaptation decision]

## Absolute application boundary

Industrial inspection-method evidence boundary: This industrial study does not transfer to finished natural-fibre, jute, or hemp shibari rope; visual bondage-rope inspection; rope care, cleaning, hygiene, drying, storage, inspection intervals, or retirement; knots or frictions; working loads; body contact; uplines; anchors; bondage; or human suspension. Its specimens, machinery, imaging conditions, degradation histories, and intended decisions are different. [INNOTRAC-462-2020, whole-article scope audit, pp. 55–63]

Industrial inspection-method evidence boundary: Nothing here approves, selects, rates, calibrates, installs, validates, certifies, or evaluates a camera system, rope, knot, upline, anchor, hardpoint, bondage arrangement, body-support system, or human-suspension system. No threshold, discard rule, remaining-life estimator, internal-condition classifier, or operational calculator can be generated from this corpus. [INNOTRAC-462-2020, bounded adaptation decision]

Industrial inspection-method evidence boundary: Any real inspection program requires current product-specific evidence, known construction and condition, qualified interpretation, validated coverage and error performance, and the governing requirements for its actual application. A persistent surface record can support review, but it cannot replace those controls. [INNOTRAC-462-2020, bounded adaptation decision]

Industrial inspection-method evidence boundary: Gregor Novak's 2020 article is licensed CC BY 4.0. This corpus is a manually rewritten, narrowed, component-filtered adaptation made on 2026-07-16; it credits the author and source, records the license, and does not imply endorsement. Patent and trademark rights are outside the license, so products, trademarks, logos, and uncertain components are excluded. [CC-BY-4.0-LEGALCODE, scope and attribution surfaces]

source_label: Kink Education Code of Conduct (KECC), version 1
text:
# Kink Education Code of Conduct v. 1: educator and producer ethics framework

## Attribution, licence, and adaptation status

This is a manually adapted and condensed study document based on *The Kink Education Code of Conduct (KECC), version 1*, created by the v. 1 KECC Collective. The reviewed source is available as a [full web version](https://www.thekecc.org/fullVersion.html) and a [PDF](https://www.thekecc.org/KECC.pdf). The PDF snapshot is dated 25 April 2019. The source work and this adaptation are licensed under the [Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International licence](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/). [KECC-HOME: Welcome; KECC-PDF: visible document date; CC-DEED: Attribution and ShareAlike]

Changes made: the source was manually selected, paraphrased, condensed, reorganized, and annotated with scope and safety boundaries. Navigation, individual contributor names, contact details, URLs as factual trivia, repeated wording, unrelated examples, and procedural kink techniques were omitted. No endorsement by the KECC Collective is implied.

The citations identify a source and a source section. `KECC-HOME` means the reviewed welcome page, `KECC-FULL` means the reviewed full-version HTML, and `KECC-PDF` means the reviewed PDF. The source ledger maps those identifiers to exact retrieval records. Licence references are rights metadata, not substantive ethics claims.

## Version and authority boundary

This is the Collective's first version, not a timeless or universal code. The PDF says version 1 was incomplete, expected practices and terminology to change, and anticipated later revision. It also acknowledges that the Collective did not represent the full diversity of the kink community as well as it wanted to. The reviewed sources therefore provide a historically situated ethics proposal from a named collective of educators and producers, not proof of present-day consensus. [KECC-HOME: Welcome; KECC-PDF: Frequently Asked Questions]

The KECC calls itself a practical roadmap rather than a precise legal code. It asks readers to apply judgment and good faith to the spirit of its commitments. The Collective did not claim to monitor adopters or centrally enforce compliance; adopters remained responsible for their own alignment and accountability processes. Adoption did not mean perfection, membership in the Collective, professional certification, or a guarantee that a class, educator, producer, venue, relationship, or activity was safe. [KECC-FULL: Introduction; KECC-PDF: Frequently Asked Questions and Adopting the KECC]

The source's use of words such as *professional*, *qualified*, *expert*, *high risk*, *safe*, or *best practice* does not create a licence, credential, clinical judgment, legal duty, or safety certification. The source says professionalism is an ethical mindset rather than a measure of income, and it explicitly recognizes that much kink education is delivered by expert amateurs because formal standards do not cover many kink topics. [KECC-HOME: Welcome; KECC-FULL: Professional competence — Discussion]

## How the document assigns responsibilities

Every main topic in the full version has three perspectives. The **educator** part addresses anyone who teaches and may sometimes inform leaders or performers. The **producer** part addresses people or organizations that hire or host educators. The **discussion** part explains the rationale, tensions, and harms the proposed conduct is intended to address. These roles should not be collapsed: an educator controls their teaching and personal conduct, while a producer controls selection, event policy, staffing, reporting structures, and institutional support. [KECC-FULL: Introduction — Organization]

The code is organized around a recurring systems idea. Students learn not only from explicit instruction but also from the conduct that educators and venues normalize. Booking confers status and access, classroom audiences add social pressure, and organizational silence can look like approval. Ethical education therefore depends on what educators model, what producers enable, how both disclose limits, and whether people can report harm without coercion or retaliation. [KECC-HOME: Welcome; KECC-FULL: Modeling consent — Discussion; Professional conduct — Discussion]

## Modeling consent

### Educator commitments

Version 1 describes excellent consent as informed agreement among people who understand their own capacity and the capacity of others involved. Agreement should be explicit or enthusiastic, free from coercion, force, and manipulation, and treated as continuous rather than a one-time permission. Doubt or confusion is a stop signal. The code distinguishes the form that agreement may take in public teaching or play from private situations, but it does not turn relationship history into unlimited permission. [KECC-FULL: Modeling consent — Educator]

source_label: Kink Education Code of Conduct (KECC), version 1
text:
Classroom touch carries no implied permission. The educator is expected to obtain specific verbal agreement from each person before touching them, avoid initiating a new negotiation in front of the classroom audience, include consent in every class, and give observers enough context to understand that a demonstration is consensual. Prior negotiation with a regular demonstration partner may be described or partially reenacted so the audience sees the consent process rather than merely being told it occurred. [KECC-FULL: Modeling consent — Educator and Discussion]

The duty to model consent is not limited to a scripted classroom moment. Version 1 argues that students also learn from an educator's public and private conduct, so a teacher who normalizes careless consent outside class can undermine what they teach inside it. This is the source's ethical position, not a claim that every aspect of private life is open to public inspection. [KECC-FULL: Modeling consent — Discussion]

### Producer commitments

The producer perspective places consent throughout the educational program rather than in a single opening statement. Producers ask educators to address consent in every class, create programs and policies that help attendees practice it, and hold staff and educators to a heightened standard while providing support to meet that standard. [KECC-FULL: Modeling consent — Producer]

### Why visible modeling matters

The discussion distinguishes *having* consent from making consent legible to students. A private negotiation may be ethically sufficient between participants yet pedagogically opaque to observers. In a class, the educator also has to prevent the audience from reasonably mistaking a demonstration for a violation or learning that consent is assumed whenever a teacher appears confident. [KECC-FULL: Modeling consent — Discussion]

## Student demonstrations: power, selection, and care

### The risk created by the classroom

Version 1 treats use of current students as demonstration partners as intrinsically high risk because educator status and classroom attention create a substantial power differential. Social pressure can make refusal or withdrawal difficult, especially after a demonstration starts. Pressure to be entertaining can make an instructor miss discomfort, and the apparent selection of volunteers for attractiveness can alienate other students. A nominally voluntary response is therefore not enough by itself to resolve the setting's power dynamics. [KECC-FULL: Using students for demos — Discussion]

### Educator preference order

The proposed preference order is to use another educator or an existing play partner first, arrange and negotiate with a suitable volunteer well before class second, and accept an in-class student volunteer only as a last resort with additional safeguards. Advance negotiation is to be written, specific about what the person is volunteering for, and specific about relevant risks. The hierarchy reduces situational pressure; it does not certify any demonstration as safe. [KECC-FULL: Using students for demos — Educator]

### Safeguards when students may volunteer

The educator is expected to announce in the class description and at the beginning that volunteers may be requested. Before asking, the educator explains what the demonstration entails and its risks. They do not call on a particular person, repeatedly pressure the room, allow one attendee to volunteer another, or tolerate heckling and coercion. If no one volunteers promptly, the demonstration is skipped. [KECC-FULL: Using students for demos — Educator and Discussion]

Selection should require unambiguous, enthusiastic agreement and the person's ability to perform what the demonstration requires. The volunteer may stop for any reason at any time. Safety, consent, and relevant ability are the primary selection criteria, while the source also encourages inclusive representation across gender, race, age, orientation, body shape, and physical ability. An existing power-exchange relationship does not erase the classroom issue; if it affects how someone was volunteered, the source says that context should be explained to the class. [KECC-FULL: Using students for demos — Educator and Discussion]

The educator accepts responsibility for care after the demonstration, remains available to provide needed aftercare, and has a formal plan communicated to the venue and class. The source uses *aftercare* as a required planning category but does not make one response appropriate for every person or teach a care procedure. [KECC-FULL: Using students for demos — Educator]

### Producer safeguards

Producers set clear standards for student demonstrations, make sure educators understand those standards, encourage advance selection and negotiation, and actively support a student who has a bad experience. The discussion expressly invites producers and educators to adopt rules more restrictive than version 1 when their circumstances warrant it. [KECC-FULL: Using students for demos — Producer and Discussion]

## Competence, qualifications, and personal risk profiles

### Teaching inside one's competence

source_label: Kink Education Code of Conduct (KECC), version 1
text:
An educator should teach only material they understand thoroughly and can teach correctly. If their approach departs from commonly accepted practice, they disclose that difference and explain the reasoning. When teaching a subject that belongs partly to a regulated or specialized profession—such as anatomy, mental health, trauma response, or legal and medical topics—they identify whether they possess relevant professional qualifications, had the material reviewed by a qualified professional, or are offering personal opinion. [KECC-FULL: Professional competence — Educator]

That disclosure does not turn professional review into endorsement of the whole class, and personal experience is not presented as a credential. The source explains that students often lack enough domain expertise to evaluate a teacher's claims independently, making accurate information and candid disclosure of limits central to informed choice. [KECC-FULL: Professional competence — Discussion]

### Producer evaluation

Producers seek educators with topic-specific expertise, distinguish confidence from competence, check that teaching stays within the person's competence, and require transparent credential disclosure. Version 1 rejects using educators who falsify or misrepresent credentials. No credential listed in a biography proves good consent, sound judgment, or complete safety; producer evaluation remains a broader process. [KECC-FULL: Professional competence — Producer]

### Respecting different risk decisions

The educator does not pressure a student to exceed that student's risk profile. Students may observe rather than perform an activity. For material the educator considers high risk, the source calls for clear risk communication and in-class negotiation before hands-on practice, along with respect for the venue's policies. These are autonomy and disclosure commitments, not a risk-ranking system or authorization to teach a particular technique. [KECC-FULL: Professional competence — Educator]

The code's discussion recognizes a structural problem: formal professional standards do not exist for many kink activities, and professionals in adjacent fields may have limits on what they can teach. It therefore expects diligence and honest limits from expert amateurs rather than inventing a certification the field does not possess. [KECC-FULL: Professional competence — Discussion]

## Inclusion, accessibility, and the learning environment

### Educator responsibilities

Version 1 asks educators to treat people respectfully and not discriminate on protected or marginalized characteristics, retaliate for a discrimination complaint, or create a hostile environment. When their conduct causes such an environment, they should apologize and correct course. Educators use a person's stated pronouns, ask when unsure, intervene around classroom microaggressions, and avoid imposing gender roles except where a genuinely gender-specific topic requires that context. [KECC-FULL: Inclusion — Educator]

Teaching should work across genders, body types, and physical abilities whenever possible. Educators should support different learning needs and actively develop ways for students with those needs to learn. Inclusivity is not merely demographic appearance: it affects examples, language, volunteer selection, physical participation, instructional format, and the ability to audit rather than perform. [KECC-FULL: Inclusion — Educator; Using students for demos — Educator]

The source describes microaggressions as indirect or subtle discriminatory acts whose harmful character does not depend on hostile intent. It distinguishes overt derogation, communications that demean a marginalized person, and statements that minimize or invalidate that person's experience. The useful teaching principle is to respond to effect and context, not to treat lack of intent as automatic resolution. The source's culture-specific examples are omitted because they are illustrative, not kink techniques or universal diagnostic tests. [KECC-FULL: Inclusion — Discussion]

### Producer responsibilities

Producers seek diversity among staff and educators, include varied perspectives in decisions, consider inclusion in event design and promotion, and remain open to feedback. Version 1 encourages tracking and publishing diversity information when feasible but acknowledges that not every organization can do so. [KECC-FULL: Inclusion — Producer and Discussion]

The event-level commitments include a published conduct code, refusal to tolerate bigotry or harassment, a formal route for reporting consent incidents and other problems, and at least one staff member trained to respond. The producer also avoids working with people whose beliefs are incompatible with fair treatment. The discussion recommends plans to support people who are triggered or experience trauma and training staff in diversity and inclusion when possible. [KECC-FULL: Inclusion — Producer and Discussion]

These provisions describe an aspirational event structure. A published policy or trained staff role does not prove accessibility, impartial handling, medical competence, legal compliance, or safety; actual implementation and community feedback still matter.

## Accountability contacts and circles

### Accountability as a learning process

Version 1 defines accountability as a process for addressing and repairing conduct that conflicts with stated values and commitments. It assumes people will sometimes fail, so the relevant questions include whether they invite feedback, acknowledge a mistake, apologize, repair what can be repaired, learn, and change patterns. Accountability is not equated with claiming perfection. [KECC-FULL: Accountability — Educator and Discussion]

source_label: Kink Education Code of Conduct (KECC), version 1
text:
An educator proactively seeks feedback from partners, students, peers, and community leaders; responds honestly to reports of injury or consent problems; participates in a relevant accountability process; and follows the processes of producers and organizations with which they work. They publicly identify their voluntary commitment and make an accountability contact available. The source also opposes nondisclosure agreements or threats of legal action used to prevent partners from discussing their experiences. [KECC-FULL: Accountability — Educator]

A producer likewise makes its commitment and contact route visible, gives that contact information to students and attendees, and publishes or develops a written process covering staff, educators, and attendees. Version 1 does not supply a central tribunal, investigation standard, sanction table, or proof that any local process is fair. [KECC-FULL: Accountability — Producer; KECC-PDF: Frequently Asked Questions]

### Contact and circle roles

In the source's provisional vocabulary, an **accountability contact** is a trusted intake person for safety or consent concerns and a repository for incident information. An **accountability circle** is a group that advises and, when needed, coordinates an accountability process. A circle member need not be a contact, and an individual or organization may have several contacts. The source warns that terminology was not consistent across the community and expected this area to evolve. [KECC-FULL: Accountability — Discussion]

The design suggestions try to reduce conflicts and blind spots. At least one contact should be independent of close romantic, play, friendship, or business ties. Multiple contacts let a reporter avoid someone they do not trust. Contacts and circle members should be diverse, approachable, and trusted by both the subject and community. One designated repository can help identify patterns across reports, while sensitive information should remain limited to people with a compelling need to know. [KECC-FULL: Accountability — Discussion; Professional conduct — Discussion]

These roles do not themselves establish investigator competence, due process, confidentiality, independence, or safety. They are a proposed communication and memory structure. Local policy must still address conflicts of interest, access, retention, response, support, proportionality, and the privacy tension discussed below.

## Boundaries between educators and students

### Conduct during teaching

The educator maintains a professional demeanor in public classes and private lessons. During class they do not cruise or engage in recreational play with a student with whom they have no existing relationship, and they explain existing partner agreements when those agreements provide necessary context for classroom behavior. They follow venue rules and maintain a formal policy for sex, play, or romance with students, developed with input beyond themselves. [KECC-FULL: Boundaries with students — Educator]

If an educator considers a relationship with a current or former student, version 1 calls for exceptional care, explicit discussion of the educational power differential and its consent risks, and scrupulous consent practice. Mentoring begins with negotiated boundaries around play, sex, and romance; any expectation that play or sex is exchanged for mentoring must be disclosed before the relationship begins. Disclosure does not by itself remove coercion or make an exchange ethical. [KECC-FULL: Boundaries with students — Educator]

### Policy choice and contextual risk

The Collective did not impose an absolute prohibition in version 1, but said a blanket prohibition would be preferable in most cases and strongly recommended that educators and producers consider it. The discussion identifies combined teacher/student and dominance/submission power differences, alienation of other students, reputational effects, status trading, and predatory access as concerns. Circumstances such as class intimacy and duration can change the risk. [KECC-FULL: Boundaries with students — Discussion]

Producers create and publish clear written standards and give them to educators before teaching. The discussion also applies conflict-of-interest reasoning to producers' relationships with educators: hiring a partner is not automatically unethical, but exchanging professional opportunity for sex or play is. [KECC-FULL: Boundaries with students — Producer and Discussion]

## Professional conduct and bidirectional vetting

### Educator candor and reliability

The educator gives complete, accurate answers during booking; represents experience and qualifications honestly; states logistical and compensation expectations clearly; arrives prepared and on time; and recognizes that conduct while working reflects on the host. [KECC-FULL: Professional conduct — Educator]

### Producer selection and support

Version 1 tells producers to use a real vetting process for staff and visiting educators, consider both professional and personal misconduct relevant to the role, and reject patterns of consent violation, manipulation, malice, or deception. New educators should receive coaching and responsibilities proportionate to developing expertise. When a producer chooses someone with a controversial consent history, the source favors accurate disclosure sufficient for attendees to make an informed choice. [KECC-FULL: Professional conduct — Producer]

source_label: Kink Education Code of Conduct (KECC), version 1
text:
Producers listen respectfully to community reports, take concerns seriously, and maintain a response process. They also treat educators fairly, communicate expectations and commitments, keep those commitments, share policies that exceed the KECC, and help educators understand local standards. Version 1 asks for an event policy concerning attendees known for consent or safety problems but does not prescribe a sanction, investigation, or exclusion procedure. [KECC-FULL: Professional conduct — Producer]

### Information used in vetting

The suggested intake topics include an educator's accountability contact; significant injuries; accusations of consent violation; accountability processes; exclusions from events; changes made to prevent recurrence; approach to consent and demonstration partners; subject-matter experience; qualifications; teaching experience; and whether they follow the code. These are disclosure prompts, not a scoring rubric. A denial, reference, credential, or completed questionnaire does not prove absence of risk. [KECC-FULL: Professional conduct — Discussion]

References supplied by an applicant are not sufficient on their own. The source recommends consulting more independent sources, including prior education coordinators. Information gathered during vetting should be handled confidentially and restricted to people with a compelling need. Individual network names and links from the source are omitted because they are not enduring conduct principles. [KECC-FULL: Professional conduct — Discussion]

Vetting runs in both directions. Educators may ask venues and producers about consent and accountability policies because teaching there can look like endorsement of the venue. Likewise, booking an educator gives that person status, access, and an apparent institutional endorsement. Both sides should therefore examine the relationship they are legitimizing, not only the class outline. [KECC-FULL: Professional conduct — Discussion]

## Disclosure, incident history, and privacy

### Educator disclosure and non-retaliation

Version 1 asks educators to give producers complete and accurate information about consent incidents they caused or were accused of, injuries they caused or were accused of, accountability processes focused on them, and sanctions or exclusions from kink groups. When disclosing, they protect the privacy of people who may have been harmed. The wording deliberately includes allegations as well as concluded findings; disclosure is not the same as adjudicating truth. [KECC-FULL: Disclosure and privacy — Educator]

Educators do not pressure people to remain silent, demand that partners refrain from discussing their experiences, or retaliate when concerns are shared directly or through others. They also respect private forums: they do not solicit information they should not receive, consume improperly shared information, or harass and interrogate people for discussing them privately. [KECC-FULL: Disclosure and privacy — Educator]

### Producer handling

Producers protect identifying information about attendees, restrict private material to people with a specific need, and put the privacy and safety of harmed people first when warning other producers about a potentially harmful person. At the same time, version 1 asks producers to be as open as possible about consent incidents or injuries at their events while protecting attendee privacy. [KECC-FULL: Disclosure and privacy — Producer]

### The unresolved tension

The discussion treats privacy and disclosure as a genuine conflict rather than a rule with an easy answer. Kink stigma and the intimacy of the subject can make privacy critical. Secrecy can also let a person with a harmful pattern reach new people who lack information needed for an informed decision. The source says no clear best practice had been established and expected this section to evolve. [KECC-FULL: Disclosure and privacy — Discussion]

Version 1's proposed balancing principles are to respect everyone's privacy where possible, recognize that educators accept heightened scrutiny, give greater weight to the privacy of people who may have been harmed, consider their wishes strongly, and also consider community safety, other affected people, and fairness. A person may respond to public allegations, but the response should be proportionate, non-punitive, respectful, and avoid renewed harm. [KECC-FULL: Disclosure and privacy — Discussion]

These principles do not resolve evidentiary standards, mandatory reporting, data protection law, defamation, medical confidentiality, or due process. The source is not legal advice and does not authorize public identification, private surveillance, retaliation, or disclosure beyond what is ethically and lawfully justified.

## Integrated interpretation

Version 1 can be read as a chain of responsibilities:

source_label: Kink Education Code of Conduct (KECC), version 1
text:
1. **State limits before authority is granted.** Educators disclose competence and producers distinguish expertise from confidence. [KECC-FULL: Professional competence]
2. **Make consent visible without manufacturing it in public.** Prior, specific negotiation and audience context matter; classroom pressure cannot substitute for agreement. [KECC-FULL: Modeling consent]
3. **Treat power as part of the consent environment.** Teacher status, audience attention, mentoring, booking, and institutional endorsement all change people's options. [KECC-FULL: Using students for demos; Boundaries with students; Professional conduct]
4. **Let a person decline participation without penalty.** Auditing, skipping a demonstration, and withdrawing remain available choices. [KECC-FULL: Professional competence; Using students for demos]
5. **Build inclusion into instruction and event systems.** Language, access, representation, reporting, staffing, and response design all affect who can participate. [KECC-FULL: Inclusion]
6. **Create routes for concerns before an incident.** Contacts, circles, public policies, and trained responders should exist before they are needed. [KECC-FULL: Inclusion; Accountability]
7. **Vet the status relationship, not just the syllabus.** Educators evaluate venues and producers evaluate educators because either association can communicate endorsement. [KECC-FULL: Professional conduct]
8. **Respond to mistakes with truth, repair, learning, and changed behavior.** A code commitment without a credible response to harm is incomplete. [KECC-FULL: Accountability]
9. **Hold privacy and informed choice together.** Disclosure should warn and support without casually exposing or revictimizing people. [KECC-FULL: Disclosure and privacy]
10. **Do not turn adoption into certification.** A signature, contact, policy, or questionnaire is evidence of a stated process, not proof of compliance or safety. [KECC-PDF: Frequently Asked Questions and Adopting the KECC]

## What this source does not establish

The reviewed KECC v. 1 does not establish current community consensus, legal requirements, professional licensure, a certification program, a centralized enforcement body, an investigation standard, or a guarantee of safety. It does not prove that an educator is competent because they adopted it, that a producer is accountable because a contact is listed, or that a demonstration is consensual because the class was told negotiation occurred. [KECC-PDF: Frequently Asked Questions and Adopting the KECC; KECC-FULL: Introduction]

This adaptation does not teach a kink, bondage, medical, rescue, or demonstration technique. Source examples naming particular practices were used only to understand competence and disclosure categories and were not converted into instructions. Decisions about medical or legal issues require appropriately qualified, current, jurisdiction-specific help.

## Derivation and split rules

Treat the homepage, full-version HTML, PDF, this Markdown, and every derivative as one KECC-v1 source family and assign the family to one data split before chunking or question generation. Later questions may test role distinctions, consent modeling, demonstration power, competence disclosure, risk-profile respect, accessibility, accountability structure, boundaries, vetting, and privacy tradeoffs.

Do not generate recall questions about the Collective's individual members, URLs, page titles, section order, dates, file hashes, licence boilerplate, named outside organizations, or example practices. Do not turn the document into current-consensus, legal, certification, enforcement, or safety claims. Every derived answer must retain the version-1/2019 scope when it materially affects interpretation.

source_label: MAIB Report 2/2019: throw-bag rescue-line hidden fused-joint failure
text:
# Hidden fused joints in a throw-bag rescue line: a rights-filtered failure-analysis digest

## Source identity and evidence boundary

This digest covers the Marine Accident Investigation Branch report *Failure of a throw bag rescue line during a capsize drill on an unnamed rowing boat, Widnes, UK, 24 March 2018*. MAIB issued it as Report No. 2/2019 in January 2019, and GOV.UK published it on 31 January 2019. The retained evidence concerns a manufactured rescue product whose nominally continuous line actually contained hidden construction joints. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF p. 1]

This one rescue-product defect case does not validate a bondage-rope inspection or joining rule. It is a case investigation of a particular manufactured product and supply chain. It does not establish a general failure rate, certify an inspection method, or provide instructions for making or using a rope assembly in another setting. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 1, 5–7]

The source is a short casualty report, not a controlled product-population study. MAIB combined examination of the failed item, follow-up information about comparable items, a commissioned comparison of intact and joined samples, and an analysis of production quality controls. The credited laboratory details and external publications are excluded here; only MAIB's Crown-authored qualitative synthesis is retained. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 1–7]

## The apparent line was an assembly of segments

Close examination found that the rescue line was not one continuous manufactured length. It comprised several rope segments connected by thermal fusion. A connection between segments was the location that separated during the drill. The consequential discontinuity was therefore introduced during construction rather than arising solely from gradual wear of otherwise continuous rope. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 1–2] [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF p. 5]

That distinction changes the unit of analysis. “The rope” was visually presented and sold as one line, but mechanically it contained intact spans and constructed joints. The load path crossed both types of region in series, so the assembly could perform only as well as its weakest discontinuity. A property of intact rope did not establish the behavior of the completed line. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 1, 3, 5]

Thermal fusion is retained only as the report's identification of how the segments had been connected. The report evidence used here does not specify a joining process, process settings, acceptance criteria, or a method for reproducing such a connection. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 1–3, 5]

## Intact spans and joined spans behaved differently

The commissioned comparison separated intact samples from samples containing a fused joint. MAIB's analysis reports a large qualitative difference: joined samples separated under much less load than intact rope samples. Their deformation response also differed. The joint was therefore not merely a cosmetic interruption; it created a mechanically distinct class of region. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 3, 5]

The finding does not supply a transferable numerical factor in this digest. It supports the narrower conclusion that testing an intact span cannot be assumed to characterize a finished assembly containing an undocumented joint. Material continuity is part of the test article definition. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 3, 5, 7]

The final separation occurred at a joint, consistent with the comparison between joined and intact samples. MAIB accordingly treated the construction discontinuity as the failure mechanism in this case. That conclusion is specific to the examined product; it is not evidence that every joint made by any process has the same behavior. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 1–3, 5]

## The defect was not confined to the first item

Follow-up examination and notification identified other rescue lines with comparable fused joints. The report also records prior indications of similar construction discontinuities outside the single failed item. These observations make a one-off handling accident an inadequate explanation and point toward a recurring production-control problem. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 1–2, 5]

source_label: MAIB Report 2/2019: throw-bag rescue-line hidden fused-joint failure
text:
Comparable defects beyond the initiating item strengthen the inference that the joint arose before use. They do not, however, reveal the prevalence of the defect across all products. The total production population and complete inspection history were not available, and some affected items could have remained unidentified. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 2–3, 5]

This is a useful distinction between recurrence and incidence. Finding the same defect in multiple items shows that the mechanism was repeatable or repeated. It does not by itself estimate how often it occurred, which batches were unaffected, or how detection probability varied among inspectors. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF p. 5]

## Why external visual checks missed the discontinuity

The importing organization used random visual checks on finished products. Those checks did not prevent joined lines from reaching users. A line can look like one product when coiled or packed even though its load path contains connections that require deliberate exposure and examination to identify. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 2–3, 5]

Visual quality control was limited in two separate ways. Sampling left many individual products unexamined, and external viewing was not a validated test of continuity or joint behavior. Increasing the care of an inspector would not, by itself, resolve either the coverage problem or the lack of a proven relationship between appearance and mechanical behavior. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 3, 5–6]

The report therefore distinguishes a visible finished product from a verified continuous load path. External appearance can identify some gross anomalies, but absence of an observed anomaly is not evidence that every internal or concealed region shares the properties of an intact span. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 2–3, 5]

Random inspection also cannot repair an uncontrolled process. Sampling can discover output defects, but a missed sample leaves the manufacturing mechanism unchanged. When the production step capable of creating a discontinuity is not understood and controlled, the next unexamined unit can repeat it. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 3, 5–6]

## Distinct safeguards answer distinct questions

The report's quality-control discussion separates several functions that are sometimes collapsed into “inspection.” Traceability, batch control, process validation, finished-product checking, and independent verification answer different questions. None is a complete substitute for the others. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 3, 5–7]

Traceability links an individual finished item to its production history. It makes it possible to ask where and when that item was made and which production records belong to it. Without a stable item identity, later evidence about a defect cannot be connected reliably to the relevant production history. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 3, 5, 7]

Batch control groups items that share a meaningful production context. It can bound an investigation and reveal whether comparable defects cluster around common inputs or manufacturing conditions. A batch label does not show that a process was capable, and it does not prove that each item in the batch is sound. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 2–3, 5, 7]

Process validation addresses whether the manufacturing sequence consistently produces the intended continuous assembly. It focuses on the process capable of introducing the joint, rather than waiting for a finished-product sample to expose the outcome. The source does not provide a fusion-process recipe or validation protocol; the retained lesson is only that process control and output sampling are different safeguards. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 5–6]

Finished-product checks address the condition of selected outputs. They can reveal a defect that process records missed, but random checks provide incomplete coverage and visual checks may not measure the property at issue. Their evidentiary value depends on whether the checked feature has been shown to predict assembly behavior. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 3, 5–6]

Independent verification asks whether quality claims and controls receive scrutiny outside the producer's own checks. MAIB concluded that manufacturer-only quality control lacked third-party oversight and could be compromised. Independence does not replace traceability or process validation; it tests whether those controls exist, are followed, and support the claims made about the finished product. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 5–7]

source_label: MAIB Report 2/2019: throw-bag rescue-line hidden fused-joint failure
text:
Together, these safeguards form an evidence chain. Item identity connects evidence to an object; batch identity connects objects to shared history; process validation connects history to a controlled manufacturing mechanism; output checks look for departures; and independent verification challenges the adequacy of the whole chain. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 3, 5–7]

## Case-study limits and appropriate use

The report demonstrates a latent construction discontinuity in one rescue-product supply chain and records comparable defects in other items. It does not determine the full affected population, compare every manufacturing process, or validate a universal visual indicator. The laboratory comparison establishes a contrast for the examined samples, not a material-independent rule. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 3, 5–7]

The digest intentionally provides no measurements, load values, quantitative comparison factors, material specification, joining technique, rescue procedure, product action, or service rule. Its training value is conceptual: an assembly can contain hidden serial discontinuities; intact material and joints must be treated as different test articles; sampling and visual appearance can miss the relevant property; and production evidence requires several noninterchangeable controls. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF pp. 1–7]

The report identifies the Marine Accident Investigation Branch as corporate author and no individual author. It reports no conflict-of-interest statement, funding statement, or data-availability statement. The Crown-authored text reused here is attributed under the Open Government Licence version 3.0; all photographs, illustrations, tables, legal text, standards, external reports, vendor material, test details, and uncertain-rights components have been excluded. [MAIB-THROWBAG-2019, PDF p. 1]

source_label: MAIB Report 13/2017: Zarga jacketed-HMPE mooring-line failure
text:
# Jacketed HMPE ship-mooring failure: a rights-filtered failure-analysis digest

## Source identity and boundary

This digest covers the Marine Accident Investigation Branch report *Report on the investigation of the failure of a mooring line on board the LNG carrier Zarga while alongside the South Hook Liquefied Natural Gas terminal, Milford Haven resulting in serious injury to an officer on 2 March 2015*. It was issued as Report No. 13/2017 in June 2017. The retained evidence concerns an industrial ship-to-shore mooring system using a jacketed high-modulus polyethylene rope. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 1, 15 / report cover and p. 1]

This single industrial maritime failure does not validate or certify bondage rope or human suspension. The report is a casualty investigation of a particular HMPE line, vessel arrangement, operating history, and maritime load path. Its findings are not a general certification of ropes, a current survey of monitoring technology, or an inspection procedure for another setting. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 15, 98–111 / report pp. 1, 84–97]

The report uses HMPE as a convenient label for high-modulus polyethylene and ultra-high-molecular-weight polyethylene in its maritime discussion. Its subject line had a long-lay, low-twist HMPE load-bearing core enclosed by a tightly fitted braided jacket. That construction is central to the failure analysis; conclusions about it should not be detached from the material, architecture, or ship-mooring application. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 27–28 / report pp. 13–14]

## What kind of evidence the report provides

MAIB combined accident-site examination, vessel and maintenance records, operating records, internal examination of the failed line, laboratory work, comparison with an unused line, and a review of sector documents. Much of the laboratory, vendor, standards, and industry material in the factual record is separately credited. This digest does not reproduce those external components; it retains only MAIB's Crown-authored synthesis and its description of the investigation's evidentiary structure. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 54–76 / report pp. 40–62] [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 98–111 / report pp. 84–97]

The evidence has case-study limits. The investigated line was observed after failure, and some internal evidence required destructive access or specialized laboratory imaging. The report can support a causal account for this industrial failure and identify blind spots in the management system, but it cannot by itself establish population-wide failure rates, universal material behavior, or a generally validated monitoring method. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 59–75 / report pp. 45–61] [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 105–107 / report pp. 91–93]

MAIB's final synthesis treated axial-compression fatigue as the predominant mechanism in this case while also recognizing progressive contributions from cyclic loading, bending, twisting, contact, frictional effects, and prior loading history. The final tensile parting event was therefore the endpoint of accumulated internal damage, not evidence that a nominal line specification alone described the line's actual condition. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 98–100 / report pp. 84–86]

## Construction changes what can be seen

The jacket served structural and protective functions. A long-lay, low-twist core depends on its jacket to keep the rope architecture together, and the jacket shields the load-bearing material from some external contact. At the same time, it hides the core from ordinary external viewing. Protection of the core and observability of the core are therefore different properties. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 36–38 / report pp. 22–24] [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF p. 101 / report p. 87]

source_label: MAIB Report 13/2017: Zarga jacketed-HMPE mooring-line failure
text:
The jacket was not mechanically neutral. MAIB's analysis describes a tightly bound jacket applying radial compression to the core. That confinement restricted relative axial movement among yarns and fibers, made load equalization harder, and promoted local kinking and internal contact. A feature intended to maintain construction and provide external protection could consequently participate in an internal damage pathway. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 100–103 / report pp. 86–89] [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF p. 111 / report p. 97]

Low twist also mattered. Components with little helical freedom had less ability to redistribute imposed deformation. Where neighboring elements restrained a component forced into axial compression, the component could buckle sharply instead of flexing gently. Repeated movement at those localized bends could create kink bands and eventual yarn failures hidden inside the jacket. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 38–40 / report pp. 24–26] [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 99–101 / report pp. 85–87]

The report also describes HMPE as viscoelastic: stiffness and deformation response depend on loading intensity, duration, cycling, and environment. Those dependencies mean that material identity alone does not determine behavior. Construction, loading history, temperature, and the way the line interacts with the surrounding system all affect the meaning of a condition observation. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 35–36 / report pp. 21–22] [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF p. 107 / report p. 93]

## Axial compression inside a rope that is in tension

The central mechanical lesson is that a rope can be under overall tensile load while individual internal components experience axial compression. Unequal component movement, cyclic loading, twisting, or bending can force a yarn or fiber into compression while adjacent material restrains it. The whole-rope load state and the local component load state are not interchangeable descriptions. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 38–40 / report pp. 24–26] [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF p. 99 / report p. 85]

Under repeated compression, restrained components can form sharp kinks. These local geometric discontinuities act as sites for repeated flexing and concentrated contact. MAIB linked the investigated pattern of internal kinks and yarn failures to axial-compression fatigue and emphasized that this mechanism can arise from the combination of manufacturing variation, rope design, and operating application. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF p. 99 / report p. 85] [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 103, 111 / report pp. 89, 97]

Cyclic loading creates more than a repeated whole-rope tension. It can induce tension-tension fatigue while also making load distribution within a high-modulus synthetic-fiber construction uneven. At portions of a cycle where the overall load is low, individual components may still be compressed or move relative to their neighbors. The report therefore rejects a simple assumption that tensile service excludes compression damage. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 99–100 / report pp. 85–86]

The report's causal picture is interactive. Axial compression can create kinks; kinks can concentrate flexing and contact; a tight jacket can limit redistribution; repeated bending can renew those local interactions; and friction can contribute heat and internal abrasion. Progressive loss of load-bearing capacity can emerge from the sequence even when no external feature uniquely identifies it. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 99–103 / report pp. 85–89]

## Bending, contact, and the complete load path

A ship's mooring line is only one element of a load path. The investigated system included the HMPE main line, a more elastic tail, winch handling, roller fairleads, shipboard routing, the shore connection, and a variable maritime environment. Each element could change line tension, curvature, contact, cyclic response, or where a repeatedly worked section appeared in the path. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 27–31 / report pp. 13–17] [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 100–105 / report pp. 86–91]

source_label: MAIB Report 13/2017: Zarga jacketed-HMPE mooring-line failure
text:
Bending a loaded rope around fittings produces a local state different from straight, point-to-point tension. Curvature changes how components share load, while surface contact produces friction and can generate heat. Repeated passage of a section through a bend adds flex fatigue and internal abrasion to the axial-compression mechanism. Low-twist, tightly jacketed construction made those combined effects especially relevant in this case. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF p. 100 / report p. 86] [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 101–103 / report pp. 87–89]

The elastic tail also formed part of the dynamic system. Changing one component's elasticity can change the time history and cycling experienced by the main line; it does not erase pre-existing internal damage or guarantee that every component shares load uniformly. The report's system framing therefore resists assessing a line only by its nominal material and straight-line capacity. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF p. 28 / report p. 14] [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 99–100 / report pp. 85–86]

MAIB concluded that line and fitting compatibility had not been evaluated as a complete system. The installed routing repeatedly bent the line through fittings that were not compatible with the rope construction, while procurement and earlier reviews treated rope and deck equipment too independently. The report explicitly reframed mooring-deck design as a system problem rather than a collection of separately acceptable components. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 31, 104–105 / report pp. 17, 90–91]

This full-system view also changes the meaning of a component specification. A nominal value established in a different test arrangement does not capture cyclic environment, bend history, friction, tail behavior, fitting geometry, or internal construction. Fitness for a maritime load path is an interaction claim, not a material label or isolated certificate. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 102–109 / report pp. 88–95]

## Why external visual inspection was insufficient

The line received repeated external visual examination and was repeatedly described as being in good overall condition. Later internal examination found damage that the intact jacket had hidden. In this construction, an apparently acceptable cover did not establish that the load-bearing core was intact. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 15, 51–53 / report pp. 1, 37–39] [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 105–106, 110 / report pp. 91–92, 96]

Several signs of core degradation discussed in the report could be assessed only after opening or removing jacket material. That access changed or destroyed the examined section. Even internal viewing could reveal yarn-scale kinks without directly establishing the condition of molecular-scale fiber structure; specialized imaging was needed for some filament evidence. The report therefore distinguishes cover appearance, core appearance, and filament-scale evidence. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 60–64 / report pp. 46–50] [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 106–107 / report pp. 92–93]

Localized stiffness was not a reliable stand-alone answer. A tightly jacketed construction is naturally stiff across its section, and judgments about abnormal stiffness were subjective and dependent on assessor knowledge. Conflicting interpretations of stiffness further weakened its use as a unique indicator of hidden condition. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 105–106 / report pp. 91–92]

As framed in 2017, MAIB found no recognized nondestructive method capable of assessing the overall condition of high-modulus synthetic-fiber ship-mooring ropes. External vision, tactile stiffness, internal destructive examination, and laboratory imaging observed different scales and left different blind spots. This historical finding should not be presented as a claim about every technology available today. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF p. 106 / report p. 92] [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF p. 108 / report p. 94]

source_label: MAIB Report 13/2017: Zarga jacketed-HMPE mooring-line failure
text:
The operator possessed operating-hours, environmental, and terminal line-load records, yet the information was not integrated meaningfully into a condition assessment. External assessors also did not routinely receive the complete operational history they said they needed. The failure was thus not only a sensor problem; it was also a data-integration and information-flow problem. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 53–55 / report pp. 39–41] [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF p. 106 / report p. 92]

## Procurement and information-flow failure

The builder's procurement description emphasized a small set of nominal rope characteristics without supplying the full deck-fitting and working-system context. Rope suppliers did not obtain the omitted information, and the rope manufacturer was not directly consulted about compatibility with the installed fittings. MAIB treated the resulting knowledge gap as shared across builder, supplier, and operator interfaces rather than as a property of the rope alone. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 27, 31 / report pp. 13, 17] [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 108, 111 / report pp. 94, 97]

The absence of complete information persisted after installation. Operating history, environment, routing, and load data were fragmented across organizations or not supplied to those assessing the line. Earlier failure reviews also omitted the compatibility between the rope and deck fittings from their scope. A technically relevant fact can exist somewhere in the system and still be unavailable at the decision boundary where it matters. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 53–58 / report pp. 39–44] [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF p. 105 / report p. 91]

The report's lesson is epistemic as well as mechanical: a line cannot be evaluated from a procurement label when construction, fitting geometry, routing, cyclic environment, and operating history are missing. Builder, supplier, operator, and assessor each need a common system description before evidence from any one component can be interpreted. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 104–109 / report pp. 90–95]

## Why offshore test evidence did not transfer directly

The report contrasts controlled offshore tether applications with ship-mooring service. Offshore evidence emphasized controlled, relatively direct load paths and application-specific engineering. The shipboard line experienced bends, twists, repeated contact, variable cyclic loading, different environmental exposure, and a load path assembled from several interacting elements. These are different boundary conditions for the same broad material family. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 81–89 / report pp. 67–75] [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 108–109 / report pp. 94–95]

MAIB found that ship-mooring procurement had treated newer synthetic ropes too much like direct substitutes for established wire systems and had relied too heavily on test concepts developed for offshore service. A method validated for straight or controlled loading does not automatically characterize a jacketed line undergoing combined bending, twisting, contact, and cycling. Transfer requires matching construction, load path, environment, and failure mechanisms. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 101–102 / report pp. 87–88] [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 108–109 / report pp. 94–95]

This is not a finding that HMPE is inherently unsuitable. It is a finding that application suitability cannot be inferred from material identity or an offshore test result alone. The evidentiary mismatch arose because the system in service differed from the system represented by the borrowed method. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 108–109 / report pp. 94–95]

## Uncertainty and appropriate use

source_label: MAIB Report 13/2017: Zarga jacketed-HMPE mooring-line failure
text:
The report resolves a causal interpretation for the investigated event, but it also documents incomplete histories, changing external interpretations, and limitations in observing hidden damage. Its conclusions are strongest for the specific combination of jacketed low-twist HMPE construction, cyclic ship-mooring service, repeated bending and contact, and fragmented condition information. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 54–76 / report pp. 40–62] [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 98–111 / report pp. 84–97]

The digest provides no operational thresholds or service-decision rules. It supports a conceptual model: construction affects observability; overall tension can coexist with local compression; contact and cycling interact; external appearance can miss core damage; and evidence transfers only when system boundary conditions match. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF pp. 99–109 / report pp. 85–95]

The report identifies no individual author. It is published by the Marine Accident Investigation Branch as Crown copyright. It reports no conflict-of-interest statement, funding statement, or data-availability statement. The Crown-authored text reused here is attributed under the Open Government Licence version 3.0; all credited third-party components have been left out. [MAIB-ZARGA-2017, PDF p. 2 / copyright page]

source_label: Basic Statistical Properties of the Knot Efficiency
text:
# Statistical-method digest: knot efficiency as a ratio distribution

## Use boundary: this is a population model, not a rope-safety calculator

This is a manual digest of a **2022 mathematical treatment of slow-static rope breaking-strength data**. It is not validation for dynamic, cyclic, wet, aged, damaged, or natural-fiber rope; bondage; body contact; uplines; or human suspension. It does not choose a knot, assign a rating, define a safety factor, or calculate a safe load. [SYM-2022: Abstract; §1 Introduction; §4 Conclusions]

The paper studies how uncertainty propagates when knot efficiency is defined as one random breaking strength divided by another. Its probability curves describe a specified statistical model and source population. They do not turn a laboratory breaking test into an operational limit, and they do not erase uncertainty in the parameters estimated from finite samples. [SYM-2022: §1 Introduction; §2.1–§2.3; §3 Discussion]

Equations, example calculators, numerical efficiencies, portable ranges, figures, tables, knot rankings, safety claims, vendor statements, and standards or third-party text are intentionally excluded. The retained value is the reasoning: what was measured, what was calculated, what assumptions connect them, and which summaries are meaningful for a ratio distribution. [SYM-2022: §1 Introduction; §2 Results; §3 Discussion]

## Article identity and license

The source is Ján Šimon and Branislav Ftorek, “Basic Statistical Properties of the Knot Efficiency,” *Symmetry*, volume 14, article 1926, published September 15, 2022. It records receipt on August 14, 2022 and acceptance on September 10, 2022. The DOI is `10.3390/sym14091926`. [SYM-2022: front matter]

The article front matter states © 2022 by the authors and identifies the work as open access under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International. This digest attributes the authors, title, journal, date, DOI, and license, identifies itself as an adaptation, and links both source and license. [SYM-2022: front matter license; CC-BY-4.0: Attribution requirements]

## Measured strengths versus calculated efficiency

The paper labels the breaking strength of a rope specimen containing the chosen knot as **X** and the breaking strength of a straight specimen from the same rope population as **Y**. Both are directly observed destructive-test outcomes. Knot efficiency, **η = X/Y**, is calculated from them; it is not directly measured by the test machine. [SYM-2022: §1 Introduction, Definition 1]

The distinction matters because rope is heterogeneous. Different pieces from a nominally common rope population do not break at one fixed force. X and Y therefore have their own distributions, central values, and dispersions. The efficiency inherits uncertainty from both distributions and must itself be treated as a random variable. [SYM-2022: §1 Introduction]

In the model, X and Y are independent draws because testing destroys each specimen, different pieces are used for knotted and straight tests, and pieces are shuffled before assignment. “Independent” is a design and modeling assumption about draws from the two source populations; it does not mean the rope populations are perfectly characterized or that finite-sample estimates are certain. [SYM-2022: §2.1 General PDF]

## Provenance of the normal model

The 2022 paper reports that earlier work examined roughly 200 straight-rope breaking tests and roughly 80 knotted-rope breaking tests using quantile plots and two named goodness-of-fit tests: Kolmogorov–Smirnov and Shapiro–Wilk. The article says those checks did not show a statistically significant departure from normality. Those measurements are provenance from cited earlier work, not a new 2022 experimental campaign. [SYM-2022: §1 Introduction, citing prior work [2]]

Failure to reject a normal model is not proof that the source populations are normal. A goodness-of-fit test asks whether the available sample supplies enough evidence against the null model at a selected threshold. Its answer depends on sample size, test power, dependence, outliers, and how parameters were estimated. Other distributions may also be compatible with the same observations. The normal model is therefore a supported working approximation, not an established physical law. [SYM-2022: §1 Introduction; §2.2 Normal Breaking Strength]

source_label: Basic Statistical Properties of the Knot Efficiency
text:
The authors also motivate normality from maximum-entropy and central-limit reasoning. Those arguments explain why trying a normal model is plausible; they do not replace empirical validation for a new rope, condition, knot, or loading regime. [SYM-2022: §2.2 Normal Breaking Strength]

## Two common calculations that mislead

### One knotted break divided by one straight break

A single knotted specimen and a single straight specimen produce one ratio. That ratio is one draw from a potentially broad distribution, not an estimate of a sharp material constant. Which two heterogeneous rope pieces happen to be selected can dominate the result, and the destructive test supplies no repeat measurement of the same piece. [SYM-2022: §1 Introduction, misleading concept 1]

Calling this a “paired” result does not cure the problem: the two outcomes come from different pieces, not two conditions measured on one unchanged specimen. Without replication, the experiment cannot estimate either source distribution or the spread of their ratio. [SYM-2022: §1 Introduction, misleading concept 1; §2.1 General PDF]

### A ratio of two small-sample means

Using several specimens is better than using one, but simply dividing the average knotted strength by the average straight strength does not generally yield the expected value of X/Y. Even under independence, the expectation of a quotient is not the quotient of expectations. Discarding the two input variances removes information that controls the width and shape of the efficiency distribution. [SYM-2022: §1 Introduction, misleading concept 2]

Small samples add another layer: their means and variances are uncertain estimates of population parameters. The paper derives the population ratio distribution after specifying input distributions and parameters; it does not make finite-sample estimation error disappear. A responsible analysis must distinguish variability among rope specimens from uncertainty about the fitted model parameters. [SYM-2022: §1 Introduction; §2.2 Normal Breaking Strength; §3 Discussion]

## From two input distributions to one ratio distribution

The general derivation starts with continuous probability density functions for X and Y and an independent joint distribution. Every possible pair of strength outcomes maps to a ratio. Accumulating probability over all pairs that produce ratios in a small interval yields the probability density function of η. The same construction works beyond normal inputs when the required density and independence assumptions hold. [SYM-2022: §2.1 General PDF]

A **probability density function (PDF)** describes how probability is distributed across possible efficiency values. Its height at one point is not itself a probability. Probability comes from area over an interval. A narrow high peak means outcomes are concentrated; skew, multiple peaks, or long tails mean a single central number hides structure. [SYM-2022: §2.1 General PDF; §2.3 Properties]

A **cumulative distribution function (CDF)** gives the probability of drawing an efficiency at or below a threshold. Subtracting CDF values gives the probability between two thresholds. For the exact ratio-of-normal model, the PDF has an analytic form, but the CDF and many summaries require numerical evaluation rather than a short elementary expression. [SYM-2022: §2.3 Properties, item 3]

Under the normal-input special case, the ratio distribution is controlled by the relative locations and spreads of the two breaking-strength populations. The paper compresses four input descriptors into three dimensionless shape-and-scale parameters. This parameter reduction is mathematical convenience, not evidence that rope material, knot geometry, condition, and loading history cease to matter. [SYM-2022: §2.2 Normal Breaking Strength; §2.3 Properties, items 1 and 4–5]

## Model support outside the physical region

A normal distribution assigns a tiny mathematical probability to negative breaking strength because its support extends over the entire real line. A ratio also becomes extreme when the denominator is arbitrarily close to zero. The authors restrict their practical parameter sector so those nonphysical input tails are negligible, but they remain part of the exact mathematical model. [SYM-2022: §2.2 Normal Breaking Strength, Assumption 1; §2.3 Properties]

Those tails have an important consequence: the full ratio-of-normal distribution does not have a finite global mean or variance. The paper therefore defines mean and variance after conditioning on the physically meaningful efficiency interval from zero through unity. These are **truncated** or conditional summaries, not the ordinary global moments of the unbounded mathematical ratio distribution. [SYM-2022: §2.3 Properties, item 8]

source_label: Basic Statistical Properties of the Knot Efficiency
text:
Truncation solves a reporting problem only after the analyst accepts the physical interval and model. It should be stated explicitly because conditioning changes the population being summarized. A truncated mean or variance must not be confused with proof that observations outside the interval are physically valid or with a safe operating band. [SYM-2022: §2.3 Properties, items 6–8; §3.1–§3.2]

## Central tendency: mode, median, and truncated mean

The **mode** is a density peak: the most locally probable value under the fitted curve. The **median** divides cumulative probability in half. The **truncated mean** averages the ratio after conditioning on the physical interval. In an asymmetric distribution these locations need not coincide, and choosing among them depends on the reporting question. [SYM-2022: §2.3 Properties, items 8–10; §3.1 Discussion]

The ratio of the two input means is not generally the exact ratio distribution’s mean. In the paper’s “solid” approximation, that ratio is the median; the authors describe the exact-model median as nearly equal in the practical region. This limited median result does not rehabilitate the ratio of means as a universal expected efficiency. [SYM-2022: §2.3 Properties, item 9; §2.4 Solid Approximation, Theorem 5]

The exact mathematical PDF can be asymmetric and, for some parameter choices, bimodal. In the parameter region the authors regard as practically plausible, a secondary far-tail mode may carry negligible probability. Modality should therefore be checked from the fitted distribution rather than asserted from knot name alone. [SYM-2022: §2.3 Properties, items 2 and 10]

## Dispersion and the paper’s “tolerance intervals”

Dispersion describes how widely outcomes vary. The two source variances influence the ratio curve, while truncated variance describes spread only within the conditioned physical interval. Reporting a center without spread invites false precision, especially for heterogeneous or worn source material. [SYM-2022: §1 Introduction; §2.3 Properties, item 8; §3.1 Discussion]

The paper defines equal-tail central ranges analogous to familiar one-, two-, or three-standard-deviation bands of a normal curve and calls them tolerance intervals. Their endpoints are quantiles of the modeled efficiency distribution. Because the ratio curve can be skewed, the lower and upper distances from the center need not match. [SYM-2022: §2.3 Properties, item 11]

In this paper’s usage, those ranges describe a chosen share of the modeled population. They are not confidence intervals for estimated parameters, do not include a separate confidence level about population coverage, and are not engineering tolerances, safety factors, ratings, or safe-load intervals. Finite-sample uncertainty in the fitted inputs must be handled separately. [SYM-2022: §2.3 Properties, item 11; §3 Discussion]

## Exact model, solid approximation, and normal approximation

The exact ratio-of-normal PDF is algebraically complex, and its CDF lacks a short elementary form. The paper’s **solid approximation** restricts attention to a stated practical parameter sector, drops terms bounded as negligible there, renormalizes the simplified density, and obtains a closed-form CDF. That makes medians, quantiles, and central ranges easier to evaluate. [SYM-2022: §2.4 Solid Approximation]

The approximation does not make every summary elementary: the physically truncated mean and variance still require numerical integration. The paper derives upper bounds for PDF, CDF, truncated-mean, and truncated-variance errors. Those checks are part of the approximation; using the simplified form without verifying its parameter assumptions and error bounds would discard its justification. [SYM-2022: §2.4 Solid Approximation; §2.5 Error Management]

A symmetric normal approximation is simpler again.
