The discussion shows why it is case-dependent: one inherited worn-rope example is broad and asymmetric enough that the normal approximation is misleading, while one inherited new-rope example is narrow and symmetric enough for it to track the physical region closely. Neither example supplies a universal efficiency value or a rule based only on rope age. [SYM-2022: §2.5 Error Management; §3.1–§3.2 Discussion]

The two examples reuse parameter estimates associated with earlier static-rope work. They illustrate model behavior; they are not a controlled new-versus-worn aging experiment, and the 2022 paper reports no new dynamic, cyclic, wet, damaged-rope, natural-fiber, body-contact, or human-suspension validation. [SYM-2022: §3 Discussion; Data Availability Statement; reference [2]]

## What can be carried forward

source_label: Basic Statistical Properties of the Knot Efficiency
text:
The durable lesson is procedural: define the source rope population, randomize specimen assignment, measure enough straight and knotted specimens to characterize both distributions, retain both dispersions, distinguish population variability from parameter-estimation uncertainty, and propagate the two distributions through a ratio model whose assumptions are checked. [SYM-2022: §1 Introduction; §2.1–§2.3]

The PDF communicates shape, the CDF communicates threshold and interval probabilities, central measures answer different questions, and equal-tail population ranges communicate spread. None of those outputs is automatically a safety limit. The model must remain attached to its rope population, knot configuration, loading regime, specimen history, and estimation method. [SYM-2022: §2.3 Properties; §3 Discussion; §4 Conclusions]

Do not use this digest to calculate retained strength, choose a knot, set a working load, derive a safety factor, validate a body-contact system, or transfer results to another rope class. It contains no operational equations or numerical efficiency outputs. [SYM-2022: corpus scope based on §1–§4]

## Attribution and adaptation notice

Adapted from Ján Šimon and Branislav Ftorek, “Basic Statistical Properties of the Knot Efficiency,” *Symmetry* 14 (2022), article 1926, DOI [10.3390/sym14091926](https://doi.org/10.3390/sym14091926). The article states that it is licensed under [Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/). This digest changes wording, selection, ordering, and emphasis and omits equations, figures, tables, numerical examples, vendor material, third-party content, and operational claims. No endorsement by the authors, institutions, publisher, funders, vendors, or Creative Commons is implied.

## Citation key

- **SYM-2022** — Šimon and Ftorek, 2022, official MDPI PDF and XML, reviewed in full.
- **CC-BY-4.0** — Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International deed, accessed July 16, 2026.

No source HTML, XML, PDF, extracted full text, equation set, figure, or table is retained in this corpus.

source_label: Tensile Strength of Ropes and Friction Hitch Used in Tree Climbing Work
text:
# Friction-hitch and rope behavior in a 2021 arborist bench study

## Attribution, licence, and adaptation

This is a manually adapted study digest of *Tensile Strength of Ropes and Friction Hitch Used in Tree Climbing Work* by Leonardo Bianchini, Rodolfo Picchio, Andrea Colantoni, Marco Scotolati, Valerio Di Stefano, and Massimo Cecchini, published in *Forests* in 2021. DOI: [10.3390/f12111457](https://doi.org/10.3390/f12111457). The [official article](https://www.mdpi.com/1999-4907/12/11/1457) is © 2021 by the authors and distributed under the [Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International licence](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/). This adapted Markdown is also offered under CC BY 4.0. [MDPI-PDF: article identity and copyright notice; DOI: resolution metadata; CC-BY: licence deed]

Changes made: the article was manually selected, paraphrased, condensed, reorganized, and given additional transfer and safety limitations. Figures, photographs, table layouts, logos, manufacturer identities, promotional descriptions, attachment-hardware details, standard numbers and bodies, references, and operational assembly instructions were omitted. Product-model words retained below identify the two tested rope specimens only; they are not endorsements. No author, publisher, manufacturer, or institution endorses this adaptation.

Citation labels map to the source ledger. `MDPI-PDF` is the hashed official article PDF, `DOI` is the DOI identity route, and `CC-BY` is licence metadata. Licence and access metadata are not scientific findings.

## Limitations first

This was a preliminary laboratory study of two particular synthetic arborist rope systems paired with two friction hitches. It was not a field trial and did not place a person on the system. It did not test recreational rope bondage, human suspension, natural-fiber rope, rescue loads, anchors, harnesses, bodies, nerves, circulation, consent, or emergency release. Nothing in the study certifies a hitch, rope, combination, or complete life-support system. [MDPI-PDF: Abstract; §2 Materials and Methods; §4 Conclusions]

The 36 runs were slow pulls on semi-static rope in a traction bench with a short 250 mm machine stroke. The apparatus paused at fixed displacement points. This is a quasi-static, constrained bench condition. There were no dynamic drops, falls, catches, shock loads, repeated work cycles, oscillating bodies, live climbers, or cyclic hysteresis trials. The authors explicitly called for future dynamic falling-body and cyclic-loading experiments. [MDPI-PDF: §2 Materials and Methods; §3 Results and Discussion]

The authors adapted a standardized fall-protection-rope test method rather than following it unchanged. They assumed their modifications did not materially alter results, but this experiment did not establish equivalence between the modified setup and the unmodified method. The assembly also included terminal and connecting components, so the results describe the tested bench system, not the isolated intrinsic strength of a hitch or rope. [MDPI-PDF: §2 Materials and Methods]

The machine stopped after its programmed stroke. Reported loads are loads observed within that stroke, not universal breaking loads. Reported maxima are neither working-load limits nor safety factors. Statistical significance inside these data does not produce a safe rating, recommended hitch, field clearance, or prediction for other equipment. [MDPI-PDF: Abstract; §2 Materials and Methods; §3 Results and Discussion]

Only the named synthetic specimens, diameters, hitch cord, tie patterns, assembly, laboratory environment, pull history, and measurement method were observed. Different material, construction, age, wear, contamination, moisture, temperature, UV history, tying variation, dressing, loading rate, geometry, hardware, or repeated use can change behavior. The article discusses several such sources of variability and says more data are needed before larger-scale application. [MDPI-PDF: §3 Results and Discussion]

## Research question and stated purpose

The study examined how a work-rope and friction-hitch system behaved under high slow-pull stress. The authors wanted to observe load, hitch movement, rope deformation, and heat while comparing two rope models and two hitch types. They described the work as preliminary because scientific evidence for these combinations was limited. [MDPI-PDF: Abstract; §1 Introduction]

source_label: Tensile Strength of Ropes and Friction Hitch Used in Tree Climbing Work
text:
The authors expressly said the goal was **not** to define the best knot. Their purpose was to begin a series of studies about interaction between hitches and ropes and to provide comparative information for future research. Any claim that the experiment selected a universally superior hitch contradicts its stated purpose. [MDPI-PDF: §1 Introduction]

The title uses *tensile strength*, but the method primarily observed the response of assembled rope-and-hitch treatments during a bounded stroke. The article reported system load, slip, diameter change, and temperature rather than a general material strength value that can be transferred to another rope or hitch. [MDPI-PDF: Abstract; §2 Materials and Methods]

## Exact experimental factors

The two tested work-rope specimens were:

- **System A:** the article's Axis model, an 11 mm synthetic rope listed as polyester/polyamide;
- **System X:** the article's XTC 16 model, a synthetic polyamide rope listed as 13 mm in the specimen table. [MDPI-PDF: Table 1]

Both systems used an 8 mm polyester/Technora hitch cord. Manufacturer identities and declared catalog breaking loads are omitted because they are not experimental outcomes and could be mistaken for ratings of the tested assembly. The results narrative later calls the larger rope 12.7 mm, whereas the specimen table rounds or lists it as 13 mm; this digest preserves that source discrepancy rather than silently resolving it. [MDPI-PDF: Table 1; §3 Results and Discussion]

The hitch factor had two levels: **Prusik** (`P`) and **Valdotain tresse** (`T`). Crossing two ropes with two hitches produced four treatments:

- `AP`: Axis with Prusik;
- `AT`: Axis with Valdotain tresse;
- `XP`: XTC 16 with Prusik;
- `XT`: XTC 16 with Valdotain tresse. [MDPI-PDF: §2 Materials and Methods]

The labels are experimental treatment identifiers. They do not specify how to tie, dress, set, connect, or use either hitch. [MDPI-PDF: §2 Materials and Methods]

## Replication structure

There were 36 traction tests: four treatments with nine runs per treatment. The authors describe three pieces of each rope and three cords for each rope/hitch combination, with three traction tests carried out on each set of sections and cords. Thus, “nine replicates” should be read together with the reported reuse or repeated-test structure; it is not described simply as nine independently sourced, fresh, complete systems. [MDPI-PDF: §2 Materials and Methods]

The factorial identity is straightforward: two work ropes × two hitches × nine runs equals 36 tests. The relatively balanced design supports comparison among the four treatments inside this apparatus. It does not expand the number of rope models, material families, hitches, operators, environments, or loading modes represented. [MDPI-PDF: §2 Materials and Methods]

## Bench protocol without an operational recipe

Each run used a traction machine with a 250 mm available stroke. The study recorded an initial state and five later positions separated by 50 mm of machine travel. The machine was stopped at each observation point. This digest retains displacement and sampling cadence because they define the evidentiary window, but omits terminal-knot construction, preloading procedure, connector arrangement, and equipment brands so the paper is not repurposed as a climbing or suspension recipe. [MDPI-PDF: §2 Materials and Methods]

At the displacement points, researchers measured:

- applied tensile load on the assembled system, `C`, in kilonewtons;
- friction-hitch slip along the work rope, `S`, in centimeters;
- change in work-rope diameter, `Δf`, in millimeters. [MDPI-PDF: §2 Materials and Methods]

They also measured temperature near the hitch at the beginning and end of a run with thermal imaging. Ambient temperature and humidity were recorded before testing. Temperature was therefore an endpoint comparison, not a continuous measurement of a rapid thermal peak. [MDPI-PDF: §2 Materials and Methods]

The experiment did not continuously measure internal fiber temperature, melt onset, body motion, fall distance, impact force, energy absorption, recovery between cycles, or long-term wear. A small endpoint temperature change under slow pull cannot establish that heating is harmless under a fast slide or catch. [MDPI-PDF: §2 Materials and Methods; §3 Results and Discussion]

## Statistical plan

source_label: Tensile Strength of Ropes and Friction Hitch Used in Tree Climbing Work
text:
The authors checked distributional assumptions and variance homogeneity before their main comparisons. Ambient conditions and start-to-end rope temperature were assessed with one-factor analysis of variance. The main pulling outcomes were analyzed with factorial multivariate analysis of variance using rope (`A` or `X`) and hitch (`P` or `T`) as factors and load, slip, and diameter change as responses. [MDPI-PDF: §2 Statistical Analysis]

Post-hoc treatment comparisons used a family-wise 95% confidence level with an analysis threshold of `p = 0.05`. Regression models examined load as a function of slip and diameter change, and diameter change as a function of slip. These models describe covariation in the observed bench data; they are not field-load calculators. [MDPI-PDF: §2 Statistical Analysis]

The statistical unit and the physical specimen structure must be kept visible together. Multiple displacement observations came from each pull, and repeated pulls were described across a limited set of rope pieces and cords. A large number of recorded points does not create the material diversity of a large independent equipment sample. [MDPI-PDF: §2 Materials and Methods; §2 Statistical Analysis]

## Observed system-level extrema

Across all recorded observations, the abstract reports an observed maximum load of 18.7 kN, maximum hitch slip of 9.6 cm, and maximum diameter change of 3 mm. These are extrema from this apparatus and test window. They must not be relabeled as minimum breaking strengths, safe working loads, rescue ratings, user-weight limits, or acceptance criteria. [MDPI-PDF: Abstract]

The mean load trends at the end of the stroke were reported as approximately 11.9 kN for `AP`, 15.1 kN for `XP`, 9.3 kN for `AT`, and 8.6 kN for `XT`. The individual 18.7 kN maximum and these treatment means describe different summaries; neither is a rating. [MDPI-PDF: §3 Results and Discussion]

The Valdotain-tresse treatments showed more movement by the final point: about 7.17 cm for `AT` and 8.97 cm for `XT`. `AP` was reported at about 4.73 cm, roughly half the larger values. For diameter change at the final point, `AT` had the smallest reported mean reduction, about 1.72 mm, while `XP` had the largest, about 3 mm. These are treatment observations under the same bounded stroke, not tolerances for field use. [MDPI-PDF: §3 Results and Discussion]

## Hitch-related relationships

In the factorial analysis, hitch type had a statistically significant effect on load and on slip, with reported `p < 0.001` for each. Within these systems and this stroke, the Prusik treatments developed greater grip, produced higher machine load, and slipped less than the Valdotain-tresse treatments. The authors interpreted this pattern as greater friction from the Prusik. [MDPI-PDF: §3 Results and Discussion; Table 3]

That comparative statement does not mean “more grip is safer” or “less slip is better” in every system. Greater grip co-occurred with greater load and more diameter reduction in parts of this experiment. A hitch can change several coupled outcomes at once, and this study did not define an optimal balance for a person-support application. [MDPI-PDF: §3 Results and Discussion]

The effect emerged as the short pull progressed; the four treatments were not clearly separated at the earliest observation. The relationship therefore belongs to the tested displacement history, rather than being a single context-free property attached to a hitch name. [MDPI-PDF: §3 Results and Discussion]

## Rope-related relationships

Rope type had a statistically significant effect on diameter change, reported at `p < 0.001`. The larger XTC 16 specimen showed greater diameter deformation than the Axis specimen, regardless of hitch in the authors' summary. They suggested that material, braid, and diameter might contribute, but the two-rope experiment cannot isolate which of those correlated specimen differences caused the effect. [MDPI-PDF: §3 Results and Discussion; Table 3]

Because each rope model bundles several properties—nominal diameter, material description, construction, and model-specific geometry—the rope factor is not a clean test of diameter alone or polymer alone. “Rope type mattered for deformation” is supported; “this polymer or diameter always causes the difference” is not. [MDPI-PDF: Table 1; §3 Results and Discussion]

source_label: Tensile Strength of Ropes and Friction Hitch Used in Tree Climbing Work
text:
The paper's broader discussion mentions environmental deterioration, material variation, braid geometry, and nonlinear time-dependent synthetic-rope behavior, but those issues were not experimentally crossed in the four treatments. Literature discussion is not evidence that this bench dataset measured those factors. [MDPI-PDF: §3 Results and Discussion]

## Regression relationships

Separate regressions reported adjusted coefficients of determination of about `0.445` for load versus diameter reduction, `0.418` for load versus hitch slip, and `0.378` for diameter reduction versus slip. A combined model using slip and diameter reduction to describe load had adjusted `R² = 0.535`. [MDPI-PDF: §3 Results and Discussion; Tables 4–5]

The slip relationships included nonlinear terms, so the coefficients should not be reduced to a universal straight-line conversion. The adjusted `R²` values indicate partial explanation within the observed data; substantial variation remained. They do not validate extrapolation beyond the stroke, predict failure, or establish causality among load, slip, and deformation. [MDPI-PDF: Tables 4–5]

The authors summarized the coupled pattern as follows: more hitch friction corresponded to greater load and less slip, while less friction corresponded to lower load and more slip; diameter deformation was associated more strongly with rope type. This is the defensible comparative lesson when it remains attached to the exact specimens and quasi-static method. [MDPI-PDF: §3 Results and Discussion]

## Temperature observations

The reported start-to-end temperature differences near the hitch were approximately 6.2 °C (`AP`), 6.8 °C (`AT`), 6.7 °C (`XP`), and 3.1 °C (`XT`). The one-factor analysis did not find a significant difference among treatments. Ambient temperature and humidity also did not show a significant treatment effect in the authors' analysis. [MDPI-PDF: §3 Results and Discussion]

“No significant difference among these four endpoint changes” is not the same as “no heating occurred” or “temperature is unimportant.” All four treatments had a measured change, the method sampled only the beginning and end, and there was no dynamic slide, fall, or repeated cycle. The paper itself distinguishes its static data from dynamic events and calls for further testing. [MDPI-PDF: §2 Materials and Methods; §3 Results and Discussion]

## What the experiment supports

The study supports these limited conclusions:

1. A balanced `2 × 2` slow-pull bench experiment can reveal coupled differences in load, hitch slip, rope deformation, and endpoint temperature among specified rope/hitch treatments. [MDPI-PDF: §2 Materials and Methods]
2. In these treatments, hitch type was associated primarily with load and slip, while rope type was associated primarily with diameter deformation. [MDPI-PDF: §3 Results and Discussion]
3. The Prusik treatments gripped more, carried higher observed machine load, and moved less than the Valdotain-tresse treatments over the short stroke. [MDPI-PDF: §3 Results and Discussion]
4. The XTC 16 specimen deformed more in diameter than the Axis specimen in this experiment. [MDPI-PDF: §3 Results and Discussion]
5. Load, slip, and diameter deformation were related but none of the reported regression models fully explained the other outcomes. [MDPI-PDF: Tables 4–5]
6. The preliminary comparison raised research questions; it did not identify a best knot. [MDPI-PDF: §1 Introduction; §4 Conclusions]

## What the experiment does not support

The study does not support any of the following:

- a breaking-strength value for every Prusik, Valdotain tresse, Axis, XTC 16, or hitch cord;
- a safe working load, user-weight limit, safety factor, retirement threshold, or inspection rule;
- a recommendation to choose the hitch that showed more grip, less slip, or higher load;
- a field claim about falls, dynamic catches, repeated cycles, cyclic hysteresis, shock loading, rescue, or swinging bodies;
- transfer to a different rope, cord, diameter, material, braid, tie, dressing, connector, anchor, or loading geometry;
- transfer from synthetic arborist equipment to natural-fiber rope;
- transfer from occupational tree-climbing equipment to rope bondage, shibari, kinbaku, recreational suspension, or any other human-suspension system;
- a claim about anatomy, circulation, nerve safety, skin pressure, consent, emergency release, or medical response;
- certification, standards compliance, legal approval, manufacturer endorsement, or suitability for life support.

source_label: Tensile Strength of Ropes and Friction Hitch Used in Tree Climbing Work
text:
Those exclusions follow from the studied system and method, not from pessimism about the data. A well-executed narrow experiment can answer a narrow comparative question while remaining unsuitable for a different load case. [MDPI-PDF: §2 Materials and Methods; §3 Results and Discussion; §4 Conclusions]

## Interpretation discipline

When using this paper, keep five labels attached to every result: **synthetic arborist specimens**, **two ropes**, **two hitches**, **slow semi-static pull**, and **250 mm bench stroke**. If any label is dropped, the result is easier to overgeneralize.

Keep descriptive values separate from design values. An observed load is not a rating; measured slip is not an allowed limit; diameter reduction is not a discard threshold; and a nonsignificant temperature comparison is not evidence against dynamic heating.

Keep factor effects separate from causes. The hitch comparison changed load and slip, and the rope comparison changed diameter deformation, but each named specimen carries multiple material and construction properties. The experiment did not isolate every mechanism.

Keep significance separate from portability. A small `p` value addresses compatibility with a statistical null model in these data. It does not enlarge the sample to other equipment or certify a field decision.

Keep preliminary purpose separate from recommendation. The authors sought relationships and future research directions, not a best-knot ranking. This adaptation goes further by refusing to turn the observed combinations into instructions or safety advice.

## Derivation and split rules

Assign the official article HTML identity, official PDF, DOI record, this Markdown, and every derivative to one source-family split before chunking or question generation. Later questions may test the factorial design, treatment labels, measured variables, comparative effects, regression limits, temperature interpretation, and transfer boundaries.

Do not generate recall questions about author order, DOI digits, URLs, publication date, manufacturers, machine or camera brands, attachment hardware, table or figure numbers, standards identifiers, reference authors, file hashes, or citation order. Do not ask how to tie, assemble, preload, connect, climb, suspend, rescue, or select a hitch. Every answer using a numerical result must identify it as an observation from the short quasi-static bench study and must not present it as a safety rating or recommendation.

source_label: Fatigue and Weatherability Studies of Aramid-Fiber Rope Slings
text:
# Historical evidence digest: aramid-fiber rope sling fatigue and weatherability (NBSIR 76-1159)

## Use boundary: read this first

This is a manually written evidence digest of a **September 1976 prototype test program for helicopter external-cargo sling legs**. It is not a current equipment standard, an operational manual, or a validation of any rope system. The central result is that the manufacturer-installed end fittings were inadequate: those inadequate end fittings prevented a thorough evaluation of the rope bodies and left the report with observations rather than fully substantiated conclusions. [NBSIR-76-1159: title page; abstract; §8 Concluding Remarks]

Nothing here transfers the prototype findings to bondage rope, human suspension, natural-fiber rope, knots, uplines, body contact, care or retirement rules, load ratings, end-fitting construction, or helicopter operation. The source studied aramid-fiber prototype sling legs with manufacturer-installed industrial terminations. Its material, construction, termination, loading, and service context differ from those other domains. [NBSIR-76-1159: §1 Introduction; §2 Specimens]

The digest intentionally omits numerical loads, capacity ratings, loading recipes, fitting dimensions, apparatus construction details, vendor claims, obsolete procurement information, photographs, figures, and source tables. The retained value is historical and methodological: it shows how termination failures, sparse sampling, test-control anomalies, and uncombined environmental stresses can prevent apparently extensive rope tests from answering the intended material question. [NBSIR-76-1159: §3 Preliminary Tensile Tests; §4 Fatigue Studies; §5 Simulated Weathering Studies; §7 Discussion]

## Source identity and date

The report is *Fatigue and Weatherability Studies of Aramid-Fiber Rope Slings*, NBSIR 76-1159, by Nixon Halsey and Leonard Mordfin of the National Bureau of Standards. The report itself is dated September 1976 and identifies a U.S. Army air-mobility laboratory as sponsor. Its DOI is `10.6028/NBS.IR.76-1159`. The current NIST landing page lists the same authors, report number, and DOI, while giving January 1, 1976 as its publication date; the September date is the one printed on both the report title page and bibliographic sheet. [NIST-LANDING: publication metadata; NBSIR-76-1159: title page; bibliographic data sheet]

The work belonged to a program seeking lighter, easier-to-handle external-cargo slings for helicopters. This phase explored whether a then-new synthetic aramid material might be useful. It was a prototype-development study, not certification of a finished sling family and not a general study of rope. [NBSIR-76-1159: §1 Introduction]

## What was tested

The program tested 26 prototype sling-leg specimens. Each specimen combined an aramid-fiber rope body with an attached end fitting, which means the experimental object was the complete specimen rather than bare rope. That distinction is decisive because the terminations often governed the observed failure. [NBSIR-76-1159: abstract; §2 Specimens; §8 Concluding Remarks]

The specimen set crossed two rope configurations with three nominal capacity families. In the **parallel-strand** configuration, individually formed strands were gathered without cabling the collection together. In the **cabled-strand** configuration, strands were assembled around a core in a cabled structure. The report labels the capacity families low, medium, and high; this digest preserves those category names but not their historical numerical ratings. Jacket materials and end-fitting variants introduced further differences, yielding six basic rope styles and nine specimen styles. [NBSIR-76-1159: §2 Specimens; §2.2 Parallel-Strand Rope Construction; §2.3 Cabled-Strand Rope Construction; §2.4 Jackets; §2.5 End Fittings]

The ropes were aramid-fiber constructions with protective jackets. Jacket families included treated braid, extruded polymer, and untreated braid. Those differences matter as experimental confounders: configuration, capacity family, jacket, fitting, and potting practice were not varied as one clean, balanced factorial design. A result associated with one specimen style therefore cannot automatically be assigned to the rope configuration alone. [NBSIR-76-1159: §2 Specimens; §2.4 Jackets; §7.3 Weatherability]

source_label: Fatigue and Weatherability Studies of Aramid-Fiber Rope Slings
text:
All specimens arrived with manufacturer-installed potted compression-type terminations. Some fittings were commercial types associated with other rope classes; a small subset used experimental fittings. The report's own discussion warns that a fitting developed for steel wire rope or a lower-modulus synthetic rope should not be presumed suitable for aramid rope. This is retained only as an explanation of confounding in the historical experiment, not as fitting-selection or design advice. [NBSIR-76-1159: §2.5 End Fittings; §7.4 End Fittings]

## The study paths

### Preliminary tensile tests

The preliminary tests loaded specimens repeatedly and then continued to failure. Every tested specimen failed near an end fitting. Potting resin had moved beyond the fitting into adjacent fibers and then cured, removing normal flexibility at the transition. Because the termination region failed first, the tests did not determine the true strength of the rope bodies and could not support a strength comparison between parallel- and cabled-strand constructions. [NBSIR-76-1159: §3 Preliminary Tensile Tests; §7.1 Breaking Loads]

This is a classic censored-measurement problem. A machine can record a precise failure load while still failing to measure the target property. Here, the observed endpoint was often the capacity of a defective rope-to-fitting transition, not the intrinsic capacity of the rope free length. The report accordingly treated even apparently strong results cautiously. [NBSIR-76-1159: §3 Preliminary Tensile Tests; §7.1 Breaking Loads]

### Increasing-load-spectrum fatigue

One fatigue path used three successively more demanding blocks. Five specimens entered this path. Only one completed it without failure; the other four stopped early because of end-fitting failures rather than identified rope-body deficiencies. The automatic spectrum programmer also malfunctioned, so some specimens received cycle histories that deviated from the specified spectra. Both facts weaken cross-specimen comparisons: the dominant failure site was outside the intended material question, and the delivered histories were not perfectly uniform. [NBSIR-76-1159: §4.1 Increasing-Load-Spectrum Tests]

The defensible conclusion is not that four ropes failed the spectrum. It is that four complete sling-leg specimens failed, with the failures attributed to terminations. The rope-body fatigue endurance for those specimens remained unresolved. [NBSIR-76-1159: §4.1 Increasing-Load-Spectrum Tests; §7.2 Fatigue Endurance]

### Alternating-block-spectrum fatigue

The second fatigue path alternated a repeated baseline block with brief higher-load interruptions. Seven specimens entered this path: two parallel-strand and five cabled-strand. One parallel-strand specimen pulled out of a fitting prematurely, while the other completed its test. Among the cabled-strand specimens, three completed without observed failure, one sustained a partial strand failure but completed the scheduled history, and one developed a free-length fatigue failure relatively early. [NBSIR-76-1159: §4.2 Alternating-Block-Spectrum Tests]

These mixed outcomes show why survivor counts alone are inadequate. "Completed," "partial rope damage," "free-length failure," and "termination failure" represent different evidence states. Pooling them into a single pass/fail number would erase the distinction between an interpretable rope event and a censored termination event. [NBSIR-76-1159: §4.2 Alternating-Block-Spectrum Tests; §7.2 Fatigue Endurance]

Across both fatigue paths, twelve specimens were fatigue-loaded. Only one failed to endure its imposed spectrum because of damage identified in the rope itself; the others either completed their histories or stopped at an end-fitting inadequacy. That single free-length event did not establish a construction-wide weakness because another specimen of the same broad configuration and capacity family completed the same spectrum. The study also lacked high-capacity parallel-strand fatigue specimens, preventing a direct configuration comparison in that family. [NBSIR-76-1159: §7.2 Fatigue Endurance]

### Residual-strength follow-up

Seven specimens that had completed or otherwise survived their fatigue histories were later pulled in tension. Two then failed in the rope free length, although one had already shown strand damage during fatigue and the other may have been damaged by recoil from a fitting failure. The other five failed in or near an end fitting. Thus most follow-up tests again measured a termination-limited endpoint rather than clean post-fatigue rope residual strength. [NBSIR-76-1159: §4.3 Residual Strength Tests]

source_label: Fatigue and Weatherability Studies of Aramid-Fiber Rope Slings
text:
The report could not turn these follow-ups into a robust residual-strength distribution. Prior damage state differed among survivors, the survival process selected which specimens reached follow-up, and the terminations censored most final tests. A survivor that later reaches a high force before a fitting event is not proof that the rope retained a particular fraction of its original strength. [NBSIR-76-1159: §4.3 Residual Strength Tests; §7.2 Fatigue Endurance]

### Simulated weathering

Seven specimens were held under tension during accelerated cycles combining moisture, heat, and ultraviolet exposure. The weathering and fatigue programs used different specimens, so the study did not test their combined effect. The environmental apparatus also changed after the first test when unexpectedly high temperatures were observed, and the source records additional timing or airflow anomalies for individual runs. These controls make the weathering series exploratory rather than a uniform exposure dataset. [NBSIR-76-1159: §5 Simulated Weathering Studies; §5.1 Test Equipment; §7.5 Synergistic Effects]

Three specimens, all parallel-strand varieties, pulled out at the end fitting exposed inside the chamber. Their potting material had softened excessively during the simulated environment. Four specimens completed the exposure. When those four were subsequently tension-tested, only two failures occurred in the rope free length and therefore reflected rope residual strength more directly. The other failure modes did not support a clean comparison between configurations. [NBSIR-76-1159: §5.2 Tests and Results; §7.3 Weatherability]

One weathered cabled-strand specimen with a braided jacket showed visible fiber discoloration in the exposed region; the report did not observe the same change in the other specimens. That observation was based on one specimen style and was entangled with jacket differences. It is evidence of a particular historical observation, not a general weathering law or a visual retirement criterion. [NBSIR-76-1159: §5.2 Tests and Results; §7.3 Weatherability]

### Permanent elongation

The report calculated permanent or quasi-permanent elongation from free-length measurements taken around the tensile, fatigue, weathering, and follow-up tests. It observed length changes after loading, a tendency for larger changes with larger rope diameter, and a qualitative tendency for fatigue-related elongation to be greater in the cabled-strand group than in the parallel-strand group. Weathered specimens also exhibited creep-like length change. [NBSIR-76-1159: §6 Permanent Elongation]

The authors explicitly described these elongation conclusions as approximate and unsubstantiated because the dataset was sparse. Measurement opportunities and histories differed, and the design was not adequate to isolate configuration, diameter, treatment, and termination effects. Accordingly, elongation in this report is a descriptive outcome, not a service limit, inspection threshold, care instruction, or retirement rule. [NBSIR-76-1159: §6 Permanent Elongation]

## Why the end fittings control the interpretation

The report identifies several termination-related mechanisms. Potting material could migrate into the adjacent rope and cure into a stiff transition. Environmental exposure could soften a different potting system enough to permit pullout. A comparatively hard potting material could leave a sharp stiffness transition and local stress concentration even without visible seepage. Failures appeared adjacent to fittings, within fittings, and by pullout. These are distinct mechanisms, but each can prevent a test from reaching a rope-body endpoint. [NBSIR-76-1159: §3 Preliminary Tensile Tests; §5.2 Tests and Results; §7.4 End Fittings]

The study therefore does not support ranking parallel-strand against cabled-strand rope by intrinsic breaking strength. It also does not establish that either construction met a general fatigue or weatherability requirement. Most failures were directly attributable to fitting inadequacies, only 26 prototypes were tested, some cells were absent, and the remaining rope-body events were too sparse and inconsistent for complete evaluation. [NBSIR-76-1159: §7.1 Breaking Loads; §7.2 Fatigue Endurance; §7.3 Weatherability; §8 Concluding Remarks]

The authors offered cautiously favorable observations about what future, adequately terminated prototypes might be capable of, but they explicitly framed them as observations offered without fully substantiated conclusions. Those conditional statements are not evidence that the tested specimens were qualified, and they are not modern recommendations. [NBSIR-76-1159: §8 Concluding Remarks]

## Evidence that can and cannot be carried forward

source_label: Fatigue and Weatherability Studies of Aramid-Fiber Rope Slings
text:
The durable methodological lesson is that a rope test must classify *where and how* failure occurs before using the result to infer a rope-body property. Termination-limited failures are not interchangeable with free-length failures. Test-history deviations, prior damage, survivor selection, jacket differences, missing comparison cells, and separately applied stressors must remain visible in any analysis. [NBSIR-76-1159: §4 Fatigue Studies; §5 Simulated Weathering Studies; §7 Discussion]

The historical evidence supports only a narrow account: these particular aramid-fiber prototype sling legs showed a mixture of termination failures, a small number of rope-body fatigue events, a few interpretable residual-strength events, visible weathering in one specimen style, and approximate permanent elongation. The program did not isolate a universal material law. [NBSIR-76-1159: §4.3 Residual Strength Tests; §6 Permanent Elongation; §8 Concluding Remarks]

Do not use this digest to choose equipment, set a load, construct an end fitting, plan an aircraft lift, judge body-contact safety, or infer the performance of a modern rope. Any such use would exceed both the study design and this corpus's admitted scope. [NBSIR-76-1159: §1 Introduction; §8 Concluding Remarks]

## Citation key

- **NIST-LANDING** — Current NIST publication landing page for NBSIR 76-1159, accessed July 16, 2026.
- **NBSIR-76-1159** — Halsey, Nixon, and Leonard Mordfin, *Fatigue and Weatherability Studies of Aramid-Fiber Rope Slings*, National Bureau of Standards Interim Report 76-1159, September 1976, DOI `10.6028/NBS.IR.76-1159`.

This digest is a manual paraphrase. It does not reproduce the source PDF, page scans, photographs, figures, or tables.

source_label: Evaluation of Manila-Rope Fiber for Color
text:
# Historical evidence and limits: Manila-rope fiber color is not serviceability

## Source and study context

Historical source evidence: Genevieve Becker and William D. Appel authored *Evaluation of Manila-Rope Fiber for Color*, Research Paper RP627, in the *Bureau of Standards Journal of Research*, volume 11, December 1933. The article occupies printed pages 811–822 and is identified by DOI `10.6028/jres.011.057`; its closing dateline is Washington, September 16, 1933. [NIST-RP627, printed pp. 811–822; NIST-VOLUME-11]

Historical source evidence: Becker is identified as a research associate at the Bureau of Standards for the Cordage Institute, while Appel is identified as chief of the Bureau’s Textile Section. The project arose from a request by the Cordage Institute and a Federal Specifications Board committee to develop a way to evaluate the color of constituent fiber in commercial Manila rope. [NIST-RP627, printed pp. 811–812]

Historical source evidence: The paper thanks a group of cordage manufacturers and the Boston Navy Yard for furnishing experimental rope. It also describes a commercial, multi-manufacturer rope set. That is supply context for a laboratory color study, not evidence that the samples represented rope in service, the modern market, or every manufacturing process. [NIST-RP627, printed pp. 812 and 815]

Historical source boundary: “Manila rope” in this article means rope made from abaca fiber. The evidence does not concern jute or true hemp, and similarity in natural-fiber appearance does not license transfer among those materials. [NIST-RP627, printed p. 811]

## What the study observed

Historical source evidence: The article distinguishes **constituent fiber color** from **finished-rope surface appearance**. Its analytical target was a prepared fiber specimen derived from rope, not the intact outside surface that a user sees. The authors explicitly say that finished-rope surface color would be a different measurement target. [NIST-RP627, printed pp. 812–813]

Historical source evidence: The authors explain that an intact rope-surface result would depend on rope construction, yarn size, strand arrangement, twist, and dirt or dust. Surface appearance therefore combines fiber properties with construction and environmental history; it cannot be treated as a direct reading of the underlying fiber color. [NIST-RP627, printed pp. 812–813]

Historical source evidence: The study aimed to characterize fiber color before lubrication rather than the color as it actually appeared in finished rope. This difference in target matters: a laboratory result after preparation is not interchangeable with a visual judgment of an in-use rope’s exterior. [NIST-RP627, printed pp. 812 and 818]

Historical source evidence: Lubricant can change apparent color according to the lubricant’s own color, dust accumulated by it, and bleaching or darkening during exposure to light and air. The paper also found that removing lubricant did not necessarily return the fiber to its original response. These observations make lubricant and exposure history confounders, not corrections that reveal an unquestionable intrinsic color. [NIST-RP627, printed pp. 818–820]

Historical source evidence: The introduction notes that abaca fiber can be bleached, even though the authors describe bleaching as outside ordinary practice at that time. A visible lightness or darkness can therefore reflect treatment history as well as source fiber, which further blocks a simple appearance-to-quality inference. [NIST-RP627, printed p. 812]

## Serviceability was not tested

Historical source evidence: The authors state directly that their work was **not concerned with the relationship between fiber color and rope serviceability**. The paper developed and compared color-evaluation methods; it did not test whether a rope remained fit for use, retained strength, resisted deterioration, or was safe under load. [NIST-RP627, printed p. 812]

Historical source evidence: The article lists strength and resistance to deterioration influences as matters relevant to serviceability, but it does not measure those outcomes against color in this study. Those examples define what the study omitted; they are not a validated model connecting color to remaining performance. [NIST-RP627, printed p. 812]

license: Public Domain in the United States; NIST technical-series reuse permission
source_label: Evaluation of Manila-Rope Fiber for Color
text:
Historical source evidence: The authors report an industry opinion that rope made from darker fiber was less serviceable, then mention an unspecified Navy Department indication that some darker rope might perform as well as a Navy comparison rope. No design, sample description, endpoint, result table, or independently auditable citation for those Navy experiments appears in the article. [NIST-RP627, printed p. 812]

Historical source boundary: The dark-rope passage supports only a narrow historical caution: the paper itself did not establish that darkness implies poor serviceability, and the brief Navy remark prevents treating the industry opinion as settled. It does not establish that dark rope is safe, equal, undamaged, or suitable for any present use. [NIST-RP627, printed p. 812]

Historical source boundary: Rope furnished by manufacturers or the Boston Navy Yard remained material for this color study. Supply by those organizations is not Navy qualification, service validation, certification, or evidence that the study’s samples were used in the separate Navy experiments mentioned by the authors. [NIST-RP627, printed pp. 812 and 815]

## Absolute non-transfer boundary

Historical source boundary: This source supplies no color threshold, light-to-dark scale, visual ranking, measurement value, or surface-appearance rule. Its findings cannot be used to accept, reject, retire, clean, grade, or load a rope. Color alone cannot establish material identity, internal condition, strength, remaining life, contamination, hygiene, or serviceability. [NIST-RP627, printed pp. 811–822]

Historical source boundary: Nothing here evaluates or approves jute, hemp, rope cleaning, hygiene, drying, storage, retirement, working loads, body contact, knots, frictions, uplines, anchors, hardpoints, bondage, restraint, body lowering, or human suspension. No current inspection, procurement, maintenance, or safety recommendation may be derived from this historical color study. [NIST-RP627, printed pp. 811–822]

Historical source boundary: NIST’s journal page states that papers in the journal are not subject to copyright in the United States and asks users to provide attribution. NIST’s technical-series rights page reserves possible foreign rights, grants broad reuse rights to the extent NIST may assert them, and warns that third-party works or components can differ. This manually rewritten digest excludes every figure, table, product, and reference body, attributes the source, records its changes, and makes no worldwide public-domain claim. [NIST-JOURNAL-ABOUT; NIST-TECHNICAL-SERIES-RIGHTS, reviewed 2026-07-16]

source_label: A Statistical Analysis of Some Mechanical Properties of Manila Rope
text:
# Historical statistical-methods digest: three-strand manila rope procurement tests

## Use boundary: historical population analysis, not modern rope guidance

This is a manual digest of a **1947 National Bureau of Standards statistical analysis of government-purchase test records for three-strand manila rope**. It preserves provenance, specimen preparation, measurement endpoints, statistical assumptions, anomalies, and the authors’ unusually explicit sampling caveats. It does not retain equations, tables, plots, numerical strength values, tolerance values, procurement limits, or standards rules. [NBS-1947: journal pp. 551–559]

The observations describe heterogeneous rope submitted during 1938–1941, not a random experiment and not a current product population. The source does not validate modern natural-fiber bondage rope, knots, wet or aged behavior, rope care, body contact, uplines, anchors, or human suspension. It supplies no safe load, rating, safety factor, inspection interval, or retirement rule. [NBS-1947: journal pp. 551–555]

The breaking endpoint was a slow, monotonic laboratory pull on conditioned, eye-spliced specimens. It was not cyclic, impact, shock, knot, body-contact, or human-suspension testing. Only the splices were briefly immersed as a test-control measure; that does not make the results a wet-rope study. [NBS-1947: journal p. 552]

## Article identity and corrected DOI

The source is Sanford B. Newman and J. H. Curtiss, “A Statistical Analysis of Some Mechanical Properties of Manila Rope,” Research Paper **RP1847**, *Journal of Research of the National Bureau of Standards*, volume 39, December 1947, journal pages 551–559. The final page records Washington, May 26, 1947. [NBS-1947: journal pp. 551, 559]

The verified DOI is [`10.6028/jres.039.039`](https://doi.org/10.6028/jres.039.039), which resolves to the official RP1847 PDF and whose registry metadata matches the title, Newman and Curtiss, volume, issue, month, and opening page. The superficially similar DOI ending in `.037` resolves to an unrelated uranium paper and is not an identifier for this work. [DOI-AUDIT: verified and rejected resolver bindings]

## Rights status

This is a National Bureau of Standards federal technical-series article authored by Bureau personnel. NIST’s Research Library says federal NIST publications are generally public domain and not subject to copyright in the United States, with source credit requested. NIST’s technical-series policy likewise says employee-authored works are not protected in the United States and grants broad reprint and derivative permission to the extent foreign rights may exist. [NIST-RIGHTS: Library FAQ; Technical Series policy]

The adaptation credits the authors, title, series, year, DOI, and agency and identifies its changes. Source page images, tables, graphs, equations, cited third-party works, and component-level material are excluded despite the federal-text rights basis. [NIST-RIGHTS: Technical Series policy; NBS-1947: journal pp. 551–559]

## What the dataset actually represents

The abstract describes tests on more than 800 samples; the statistical section gives the exact analysis count as **863 rope observations**. The material was submitted for acceptance testing by a government agency from 1938 through 1941 and had been supplied by rope works and contractors in the preceding years, before wartime loss of manila-fiber sources. [NBS-1947: journal pp. 551–552]

This was an administrative stream of purchased material, not a probability sample designed by the authors. Popular nominal sizes appeared more often, some size groups were sparse or absent, and supply came from a composite of works, contractors, production periods, and prior treatment histories. The authors describe the implied national-output universe as far from homogeneous. [NBS-1947: journal pp. 551–552]

The source analyzes **three-strand** manila rope. Some four-strand data existed, but there was not enough for statistical treatment, so the paper expressly leaves that construction out. No inference from the fitted three-strand relationships to four-strand rope is supported. [NBS-1947: journal p. 551]

license: Public Domain in the United States; NIST technical-series reuse permission
source_label: A Statistical Analysis of Some Mechanical Properties of Manila Rope
text:
The paper attributes property variation to differences in fiber quality, method of fabrication, and previous treatment. Those factors were not separately randomized or modeled as recorded predictors; they are explanations for heterogeneity that remains folded into the observed dispersion. [NBS-1947: journal p. 551]

## What was measured

The study considers circumference, weight per unit length, and breaking strength. It examines circumference against nominal diameter, weight against nominal diameter, strength against nominal diameter, and strength against weight. These are different conditional relationships: a mean strength at fixed weight cannot generally be obtained by algebraically combining mean relationships fitted at fixed nominal diameter. [NBS-1947: journal pp. 552, 554]

### Circumference and weight

Circumference and weight were measured on an unspliced specimen after conditioning for a specified time in a controlled temperature and relative-humidity atmosphere. The specimen was placed in a horizontal machine under a prescribed nominal-size-dependent preload. Those settings are part of the historical protocol and are intentionally not reproduced as a field procedure. [NBS-1947: journal pp. 551–552]

At the specimen midpoint, a single fiber was passed snugly around the loaded rope, cut where it overlapped, and measured to obtain circumference. A specified gauge length was marked while the preload remained applied; after unloading, that section was cut out and weighed to calculate weight per unit length. Thus both properties depend on conditioning, preload, and the paper’s measurement conventions. [NBS-1947: journal pp. 551–552]

### Static breaking endpoint

Breaking tests used a separate specimen with an eye splice at each end. The specimen received the same atmospheric conditioning, after which the splices—not the full rope—were immersed briefly to reduce the chance of splice failure. Pins through the eyes transferred load in a horizontal testing machine. [NBS-1947: journal p. 552]

The protocol used one of two machine types according to the anticipated strength range, advanced the moving head at a prescribed slow rate, and increased tension until maximum load was reached and one or more strands failed. The recorded “breaking strength” is therefore the maximum static tensile load under that apparatus and endpoint definition. The paper does not present a cross-machine equivalence study. [NBS-1947: journal p. 552]

## Why 863 observations were not automatically representative

The authors explain what would have been possible with random sampling from a clearly defined homogeneous population: a large sample could closely estimate distributions and support a more elaborate empirical description. They immediately state that those conditions did not hold. They had no direct control over sampling, and the intended universe was composite and heterogeneous. [NBS-1947: journal p. 552]

They proceeded because the observations showed enough internal consistency for a deliberately simple mathematical description. That choice is conditional: the model was not supposed to provide an elaborate causal account. A large row count increased computational precision around the fitted sample pattern, but it did not remove selection bias, population ambiguity, or unrecorded production differences. [NBS-1947: journal pp. 552–555]

This is the central statistical lesson of the paper. **Sampling validity and model validity are separate from sample size.** A narrow formal error bar under an assumed model can coexist with weak knowledge about whether the fitted curve describes the target technological population. [NBS-1947: journal pp. 554–555]

## Logarithmic regression and its assumptions

The raw dispersion increased with the property mean, so ordinary unweighted regression on the original scale would not have constant variance. The authors transformed all variables to base-ten logarithms and fit simple straight-line relationships in log space by unweighted least squares. They report that preliminary exploration suggested the transformation approximately stabilized variance for circumference, weight, and strength. [NBS-1947: journal pp. 553–554]

The fitted line assumes that mean log response is linear in log predictor and that log-scale standard deviation is constant across predictor values. Converting back to an arithmetic mean requires a correction because exponentiating the mean logarithm produces a geometric mean, not the arithmetic mean. Their correction further assumes a normal distribution on the log scale. [NBS-1947: journal p. 553]

The authors favored a small-parameter power relationship that was easy to combine with the logarithmic transformation and had a physical scaling interpretation. A more flexible comparison gave a similar weight-versus-diameter fit, but that limited check did not prove the simple form exact for every relationship or subgroup. [NBS-1947: journal p. 554]

license: Public Domain in the United States; NIST technical-series reuse permission
source_label: A Statistical Analysis of Some Mechanical Properties of Manila Rope
text:
Calculations were performed with punched-card machinery: logarithms and their squares were placed on data cards, and products and cumulative sums were produced with the Bureau’s equipment. This historical implementation detail explains the computational workflow but adds no validation to the assumptions. [NBS-1947: journal p. 553]

## Means, individual dispersion, and the paper’s tolerance limits

The fitted central curves estimate a mean response for a fixed, predetermined predictor value. The paper separately estimates dispersion of individual observations about those means. Its outer “tolerance” curves concern modeled individual measurements, not uncertainty intervals for the sample means and not operational tolerances for rope use. [NBS-1947: journal pp. 554–556]

Those ranges depend on the fitted curve being the true log-scale mean, the estimated spread being the true log-scale standard deviation, and individual log responses being normally distributed. Rounding conventions and model assumptions are part of their construction. They are historical statistical summaries, not ratings, safe-load intervals, procurement rules, or modern acceptance limits. [NBS-1947: journal p. 555]

The formal standard errors of curve coefficients were small, partly because many observations entered the fit. Yet the authors say their usual random-sampling interpretation was unwarranted. They warn that unknown sampling and possible misspecification make the real accuracy of the curves as a description of the era’s national rope situation largely a matter of faith. [NBS-1947: journal pp. 554–555]

## The anomalies are evidence, not cleanup noise

The paper explicitly records three irregularities. First, several nominal-size groups not recognized in the cited federal specification had circumference, weight, and strength distributions resembling particular larger nominal groups more closely than their labels implied. The authors inferred apparent substitution or misclassification in purchasing. This is a historical data-quality finding, not a procurement rule for today. [NBS-1947: journal p. 558]

Second, observations at fixed nominal diameter tended to cluster into tighter subgroups than the overall model spread. The paper attributes this at least partly to nonrandom sampling. Batch, supplier, contract, or production structure could therefore be hidden inside a nominal-size category rather than represented as independent draws. [NBS-1947: journal p. 558]

Third, one very small nominal-size subgroup had circumference and weight observations poorly represented by the analytic fit. The authors judged global extrapolation based on the much larger dataset potentially more reliable than that anomalous subgroup, while still publishing the subgroup summary. That judgment does not validate extrapolation beyond this historical universe. [NBS-1947: journal p. 558]

Misclassification and clustering increase the pooled residual spread relative to what a cleaner within-group dataset might show. Treating every row as an exchangeable independent observation can therefore make a single dispersion estimate combine manufacturing variation, group mixture, labeling error, and measurement error. [NBS-1947: journal p. 558]

## Limits on historical and modern transfer

The data predate modern manufacturing controls and arose from government acceptance submissions during a disrupted supply era. Conditioning standardized one laboratory exposure before measurement; it did not erase previous treatment or establish aging, moisture cycling, damage progression, or outdoor durability. [NBS-1947: journal pp. 551–552]

The paper compares one strength curve with an earlier study, but those source data and plotted numerical results are excluded here. A difference between two historical procurement samples cannot by itself establish a time trend, material improvement, or current manila-rope property. [NBS-1947: journal pp. 558–559]

The durable lessons are methodological: define the population, preserve construction and procurement provenance, record conditioning and apparatus, distinguish a static endpoint from other load modes, inspect group counts, look for labeling and clustering anomalies, stabilize variance only with checked assumptions, and keep individual dispersion separate from uncertainty about a mean curve. [NBS-1947: journal pp. 551–558]

Do not derive a strength value, load limit, rating, tolerance, purchase rule, knot claim, care practice, inspection interval, or human-load decision from this digest. It deliberately contains no operational equation or numerical property output. [NBS-1947: corpus scope based on complete journal pp. 551–559 review]

## Public-domain adaptation and attribution

license: Public Domain in the United States; NIST technical-series reuse permission

source_label: NBS Technologic Paper T198: Results of Some Tests of Manila Rope
text:
# NBS Technologic Paper T198: bounded historical rope-construction and test-design evidence

Historical non-operational rope evidence: *Results of Some Tests of Manila Rope* is Technologic Paper T198 of the United States Bureau of Standards. Ambrose H. Stang, identified on the report as an associate physicist, and Lory R. Strickenberg, identified as an assistant mechanical engineer, are the named authors. The report was issued in 1921 and is preserved here only as historical evidence. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF p. 1]

Historical non-operational rope evidence: The official NIST year catalog identifies the work as T198, gives Stang and Strickenberg as its authors, and assigns DOI 10.6028/nbst.6078. That catalog also warns that its page is no longer maintained and may be out of date, so it supports identity rather than current technical guidance. [NIST-CATALOG-T198, T198 entry and page-status notice]

Historical non-operational rope evidence: The cover gives an issue date in September 1921, while the closing line dates the underlying report from Washington earlier that year. These dates describe publication history; they do not make the measurements current or establish continuity with any later rope product. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF pp. 1 and 13]

## What material the study actually covered

Historical non-operational rope evidence: The evidence concerns commercial three-strand, regular-lay Manila rope obtained chiefly through government purchase-order testing around the study period. The report says that most specimens came from rope manufacturers under purchase orders for government departments and that the accumulated test data came from the preceding few years. It therefore describes a historical procurement stream, not a newly recruited or randomly sampled product population. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF pp. 3–4]

Historical non-operational rope evidence: The reported pool contained 368 specimens, but that count does not make it representative of modern rope. Manufacturer, purchase specification, nominal size, construction, and period procurement context were entangled. The report itself says departmental purchase specifications had not all been identical and that the tests were not part of an investigational program. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF pp. 3–4]

Historical non-operational rope evidence: All specimens were described as commercial three-strand regular-lay rope. A separate subset of 18 specially hard-laid specimens supplied the elongation observations. Findings from that special subset are not interchangeable with findings from the full commercial pool. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF pp. 3–4 and 11]

Historical non-operational rope evidence boundary: The original diameter range is intentionally omitted because it could invite unsafe transfer or reconstruction of the paper's performance rules. This digest retains the construction class and sampling distinction, not the report's size-dependent outputs. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF pp. 3–4]

## Construction vocabulary

Historical non-operational rope evidence: In the report's construction hierarchy, yarns are the smaller constituent elements grouped into strands, and strands are the larger elements assembled into rope. The paper notes that yarns may also be called plies or threads in period cordage vocabulary. These are structural terms, not statements about fiber quality or suitability. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF p. 4]

Historical non-operational rope evidence: The paper uses *lay* at two levels. Rope lay is the axial distance over which the strand structure completes one turn around the rope axis; strand lay is the axial distance over which yarn structure completes one turn around the strand axis. A lay measurement therefore needs its structural level stated explicitly. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF p. 7]

Historical non-operational rope evidence: The report calls a construction regular lay when yarns turn around the strand axis in one direction while strands turn around the rope axis in the opposite direction. This is a construction description only. It does not establish strength, security, service life, or fitness for a task. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF p. 7]

Historical non-operational rope evidence: The period text associates hard-laid and soft-laid descriptions with the amount of twist used in forming strands. Those labels are relative construction vocabulary in this source, not standardized modern product grades and not permission to infer behavior from a label alone. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF p. 7]

license: Public Domain in the United States; NIST technical-series reuse permission
source_label: NBS Technologic Paper T198: Results of Some Tests of Manila Rope
text:
Historical non-operational rope evidence: The authors observed that ropes of the same nominal diameter from different makers could contain different numbers of yarns. They also reported a general tendency for smaller historical ropes to use smaller yarns. The first observation is useful as a warning that nominal external size does not uniquely identify internal construction; the second remains bounded to this historical sample. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF p. 4]

Historical non-operational rope evidence: Yarn count, strand count, rope lay, and strand lay are distinct descriptors. Treating any one of them as a complete representation of a rope would discard construction information that the original investigators recorded separately. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF pp. 4 and 8]

## Sampling, preparation, and measurement distinctions

Historical non-operational rope evidence: Breaking response was observed for the full tensile-test pool, while mass per unit length, yarn count, and rope and strand lay were recorded only for a less precisely described subset. Elongation belonged to the much smaller hard-laid subset. The resulting fields therefore have different coverage and should not be treated as a complete rectangular dataset. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF pp. 3–4]

Historical non-operational rope evidence: The laboratory used a fixed test procedure across procurement submissions, but a fixed procedure is not the same thing as controlled sampling. The report expressly says that many variables affecting rope properties were not determined because the tests were not organized as an investigation. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF p. 4]

Historical non-operational rope evidence: Tensile specimens used period-specific spliced end terminations and conditioning intended to shift rupture into the rope body and limit termination slip. Despite those precautions, roughly half reportedly failed at or near a splice. This is important negative evidence that the termination and specimen-preparation system materially affected observed failure location. No splice or conditioning recipe is retained here. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF p. 7]

Historical non-operational rope evidence: More than one capacity of test machine was used, with some smaller specimens assigned to a different machine. The report presents a common loading procedure, but machine assignment is still part of the measurement context and should not disappear when interpreting the historical records. Machine capacities, rates, fastening details, and operating instructions are excluded. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF p. 7]

Historical non-operational rope evidence: The paper separates tensile failure measurement from mass-per-length measurement and from size and construction measurements. Mass and size were assessed under a prescribed period load state, while lay and constituent counts were observed directly. These are method distinctions, not a modern test protocol, and this digest omits the load rule and all handling steps. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF pp. 7–8]

Historical non-operational rope evidence: The report derived size from a circumference measurement made around the loaded specimen rather than documenting a direct diameter measurement at multiple orientations. It does not present a modern uncertainty budget for this transformation or for the effects of irregular natural-fiber geometry. The latter limitation is an inference from what the measurement description does and does not report, not a quantified source result. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF p. 8]

Historical non-operational rope evidence: The full paper does not document modern lot traceability, botanical or geographic fiber provenance, production date, prior storage history, treatment chemistry, moisture state before receipt, randomized sampling, blinded measurement, or independent replication. Their absence prevents attribution of between-specimen variation to any one cause. [NBS-T198-1921, whole-PDF absence audit, pp. 3–13]

## Negative evidence and limits on inference

Historical non-operational rope evidence: Manufacturer-to-manufacturer differences appeared even among ropes grouped by nominal diameter. Because source, construction details, and procurement specifications varied together, the report cannot isolate a causal manufacturer effect or a universal diameter-to-construction relation. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF p. 4]

Historical non-operational rope evidence: The special hard-laid subset did not show a single constant elastic response over its observed loading path, and the authors reported no well-defined proportional limit. This is a qualitative historical result for that subset. It is not evidence that every natural-fiber rope behaves identically and supplies no modern threshold. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF pp. 11 and 13]

license: Public Domain in the United States; NIST technical-series reuse permission
source_label: NBS Technologic Paper T198: Results of Some Tests of Manila Rope
text:
Historical non-operational rope evidence: The source fitted empirical relations to its observations, but its own yarn-count discussion acknowledges substantial individual departure around a fitted average relation. The formulas, coefficients, plotted values, calculated values, error value, and every proposed use of those relations are excluded from this corpus. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF pp. 9–13]

Historical non-operational rope evidence boundary: The paper's period claim that its formulas could be used safely within a specified construction and size range is not retained as safety guidance. It depended on historical commercial material, period procurement specifications, an incompletely controlled sampling process, and test-system effects that included frequent termination-region failure. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF pp. 3–4, 7, and 13]

Historical non-operational rope evidence boundary: Tables of sample records, breaking loads, yarn counts, calculated values, government specification values, plots, equations, coefficients, and raw numerical results have all been removed. The surviving prose cannot be reversed into a breaking-strength estimate, working-load rule, safety factor, tolerance, acceptance threshold, or product ranking. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF pp. 8–13]

Historical non-operational rope evidence boundary: The two photographs and three plots are excluded in full, including captions, labels, visual comparisons, and image-derived claims. The image authorship and component rights are not established by the report, and the plots carry operationally hazardous numerical performance content. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF pp. 5–6 and 10–12]

Historical non-operational rope evidence boundary: The cited historical government specification and its requirements are not reproduced or adopted. References to purchase orders, a named specification, and period laboratory practice document context only; they are not current procurement, testing, or acceptance rules. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF pp. 3–4, 9, and 11]

## Absolute transfer boundary

Historical non-operational rope evidence boundary: This 1921 source cannot be transferred to modern rope products; 6 mm jute or hemp; rope care, cleaning, hygiene, drying, storage, inspection, or retirement; knots or frictions; working loads or safety factors; body contact; uplines; anchors; bondage; or human suspension. It did not study those materials, practices, configurations, or use cases. [NBS-T198-1921, whole-PDF scope audit, pp. 3–13]

Historical non-operational rope evidence boundary: The historical sample was commercial three-strand regular-lay Manila rope acquired around its study period, chiefly through government purchase-order testing. A shared natural-fiber label, similar diameter, similar appearance, or similar strand count does not bridge the century-scale product, process, condition, and use-case gap. [NBS-T198-1921, PDF pp. 3–4]

Historical non-operational rope evidence boundary: Nothing here approves, selects, sizes, rates, tests, certifies, installs, inspects, retires, or evaluates any rope, knot, upline, anchor, hardpoint, bondage arrangement, body-support system, or human-suspension system. No operational calculator or rule can be generated from this corpus. [NBS-T198-1921, bounded adaptation decision]

Historical non-operational rope evidence boundary: A current safety decision needs current product-specific evidence, current governing requirements, the actual condition and configuration, and appropriately qualified evaluation for the intended domain. T198 can contribute historical vocabulary and lessons about experimental confounding, but it cannot answer a present-day fitness-for-use question. [NBS-T198-1921, bounded adaptation decision]

Historical non-operational rope evidence boundary: Rights clearance is limited to eligible employee-authored federal text identified through the official NIST catalog, report title page, and technical-series policy. The policy is used only to document rights; all third-party or uncertain components are excluded. This corpus is a manually rewritten, safety-bounded adaptation made on 2026-07-16 and is republished courtesy of the National Institute of Standards and Technology. [NIST-TECHSERIES-RIGHTS, technical-series rights surface]

license: Public Domain in the United States; NIST technical-series reuse permission
source_label: NISTIR 6096 Post-Installed Anchors—A Literature Review
text:
# NISTIR 6096: historical concrete-anchor vocabulary and evidence gaps

## Evidence identity and strict boundary

Non-bondage concrete-anchor evidence: *Post-Installed Anchors—A Literature Review* is NIST Interagency/Internal Report 6096 by Geraldine S. Cheok and Long T. Phan. The official NIST record and report title page identify the work and its January 1998 publication date. [NIST-PUBREC-6096, identity surface; NISTIR-6096-1998, PDF p. 2]

Non-bondage concrete-anchor evidence: NISTIR 6096 is a historical secondary review. It surveys and organizes earlier studies; it is not a new comprehensive NIST experiment, a current standard, or a current field-qualification document. [NIST-PUBREC-6096, abstract surface; NISTIR-6096-1998, abstract / PDF p. 3 and scope / PDF pp. 11–12]

Non-bondage concrete-anchor evidence boundary: Only the NIST employee-authored review synthesis is used. Incorporated standards, third-party studies, quotations, equations, tables, figures, reference bodies, products, numerical results, procedures, and recommendations are excluded. [NISTIR-6096-1998, PDF pp. 1–109; NIST-TECHSERIES-RIGHTS, technical-series rights surface]

Non-bondage concrete-anchor evidence boundary: This digest supplies vocabulary, distinctions among research variables, historical evidence gaps, and internal source leads. It does not approve, select, size, proof-test, certify, install, or evaluate a ceiling attachment, hardpoint, anchor, bondage setup, body-support system, or human-suspension system. [NISTIR-6096-1998, scope / PDF pp. 11–12 and conclusions / PDF p. 81]

## Failure-mode vocabulary in the historical review

Non-bondage concrete-anchor evidence: The review treats failure mode as a classification of how an anchor system or surrounding concrete ceases to carry action. Its summary vocabulary includes steel failure, concrete-cone failure, edge breakout or bursting, splitting, pull-out or slip, bond-interface failure, and combinations of modes. [NISTIR-6096-1998, failure-mode synthesis / printed p. 5, PDF p. 15 and summary / printed p. 67, PDF p. 77]

Non-bondage concrete-anchor evidence: Steel failure names failure in the metal anchor component. The label identifies the component where failure occurred; by itself it gives no transferable performance value or acceptance decision. [NISTIR-6096-1998, failure-mode synthesis / printed p. 5, PDF p. 15]

Non-bondage concrete-anchor evidence: Concrete-cone failure names a breakout region in the surrounding concrete that forms around an anchorage. The review separates this substrate failure from failure confined to the metal component. [NISTIR-6096-1998, failure-mode synthesis / printed p. 5, PDF p. 15]

Non-bondage concrete-anchor evidence: Edge breakout and bursting describe concrete failure associated with proximity to a free edge. They are related edge-sensitive labels, but the review does not collapse every edge-influenced observation into one universal mechanism. [NISTIR-6096-1998, failure-mode synthesis / printed p. 5, PDF p. 15]

Non-bondage concrete-anchor evidence: Splitting failure describes cracking that divides or opens the concrete member around the anchorage region. It is distinct from a localized cone or edge-breakout label in the review's taxonomy. [NISTIR-6096-1998, failure-mode synthesis / printed p. 5, PDF p. 15]

Non-bondage concrete-anchor evidence: Pull-out or slip names withdrawal or relative movement of the anchor without treating that event as the same mechanism as metal fracture or a full concrete cone. The paired terms are historical vocabulary, not a field diagnostic rule. [NISTIR-6096-1998, failure-mode synthesis / printed p. 5, PDF p. 15]

license: Public Domain in the United States; NIST technical-series reuse permission
source_label: NISTIR 6096 Post-Installed Anchors—A Literature Review
text:
Non-bondage concrete-anchor evidence: For bonded systems, the review distinguishes failure at the anchor-to-bonding-medium interface from failure at the bonding-medium-to-concrete interface. “Bond failure” therefore does not identify one unique interface unless the observation says which interface separated. [NISTIR-6096-1998, failure-mode synthesis / printed p. 5, PDF p. 15 and summary / printed p. 67, PDF p. 77]

Non-bondage concrete-anchor evidence: More than one failure mode can appear in combination. The review also states that not every anchor type experiences every listed mode, so the vocabulary is a menu of observed mechanisms rather than a universal sequence. [NISTIR-6096-1998, summary / printed p. 67, PDF p. 77]

Non-bondage concrete-anchor evidence: A failure-mode label and a prediction model answer different questions.
